proposals.title: Comparative enzymatic profiling and tissue-specific regulatory analysis of TET2 and TET3 paralogs in DNA demethylation
proposals.motivation: The TET family of dioxygenases (TET1/2/3) catalyze the oxidation of 5-methylcytosine (5mC) to initiate DNA demethylation, a crucial epigenetic process. While TET1's role is well-characterized, the distinct enzymatic properties and precise tissue-specific functions of TET2 and TET3 remain less defined, creating a gap in understanding their unique contributions to development, cellular differentiation, and disease. Clarifying their individual catalytic profiles and expression patterns is essential for deciphering the nuanced regulation of the epigenetic landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TET2 and TET3 possess distinct, non-redundant enzymatic kinetics for 5mC oxidation and exhibit unique, tissue-specific expression and activity patterns that govern specialized epigenetic programs.
proposals.method: We will employ recombinant, purified TET2 and TET3 catalytic domains for in vitro biochemical assays using defined oligonucleotide substrates to quantify and compare their kinetics (Km, kcat) for generating 5hmC, 5fC, and 5caC. For tissue-specific analysis, we will integrate RNA-seq and Whole-Genome Oxidative Bisulfite Sequencing (WGoxBS) data from public repositories (e.g., ENCODE, Roadmap Epigenomics) using computational pipelines to correlate TET2/3 expression levels with localized 5hmC patterns across diverse human and mouse tissues. Evolutionary analysis of TET2/3 sequences will be conducted using profile hidden Markov models (HMMs) and phylogenetic methods to identify conserved functional domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, comparative kinetic characterization of TET2 and TET3, establishing a biochemical foundation for their non-redundant roles. It will map their activity to specific tissue epigenomes, offering a functional explanation for their differential involvement in developmental processes and malignancies. The integrated evolutionary analysis will contextualize their functional divergence within the broader TET family.
proposals.novelty: While TET2 and TET3 are known to be active, a systematic, side-by-side comparison of their intrinsic enzymatic properties and a data-driven mapping of their activity to tissue-specific hydroxymethylomes has not been comprehensively performed. The approach integrates rigorous biochemistry with systems-level epigenetic and evolutionary analysis, aligning with the researcher's expertise in protein function and systems biology.

proposals.title: Crystal Structure of the Human TET1 Dioxygenase Catalytic Domain in Complex with 2-Oxoglutarate and DNA
proposals.motivation: The TET family of 2-oxoglutarate (2OG)- and Fe(II)-dependent dioxygenases catalyze the sequential oxidation of 5-methylcytosine (5mC) to 5-hydroxymethylcytosine (5hmC) and further derivatives, a process central to active DNA demethylation and epigenetic regulation in development and disease. Despite the critical role of TET enzymes, the precise structural determinants governing their catalytic mechanism and substrate specificity remain poorly understood, as no high-resolution structure of a mammalian TET catalytic domain bound to its DNA substrate existed prior to 2026. Solving this structure is a fundamental prerequisite for understanding the molecular basis of epigenetic reprogramming.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the catalytic domain of human TET1 will adopt a conserved double-stranded beta-helix (DSBH) fold characteristic of 2OG-oxygenases, but will possess unique structural features—particularly in its DNA-binding loops and active site—that dictate its specificity for 5mC within a CpG dinucleotide context and explain its processive oxidation capability.
proposals.method: We will apply the established protein crystallography pipeline used successfully for other 2OG-oxygenases (e.g., PHDs, JMJD2). The human TET1 catalytic domain (residues 1369-2039) will be expressed in E. coli and purified via nickel-affinity and size-exclusion chromatography. Crystallization will be pursued using vapor diffusion with sparse matrix screens, focusing on conditions that yielded crystals for related enzymes. To trap the catalytically relevant complex, we will co-crystallize the protein with a double-stranded DNA oligonucleotide containing a central 5mCpG site, Fe(II), and the co-substrate analog 2OG or the inhibitor N-oxalylglycine (NOG). Diffraction data will be collected at a synchrotron source and structures solved by molecular replacement using the DSBH core of related oxygenases (e.g., AlkB) as a search model, followed by iterative building in Coot and refinement with Phenix.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution three-dimensional structure of a mammalian TET enzyme catalytic domain in complex with its DNA substrate, revealing the precise architecture of the active site and the molecular contacts for 5mC recognition. It will establish a definitive structural framework to interpret disease-associated mutations, guide the rational design of chemical probes to modulate TET activity, and inform models of how TET enzymes achieve processive oxidation along the methylation pathway.
proposals.novelty: While structures of bacterial homologs (e.g., NgTET1) and the TET2 catalytic domain alone were reported pre-2026, a structure of the human TET1 enzyme engaged with 5mC-DNA has remained elusive. This proposal directly targets this gap, applying a classic structural biology approach to a high-impact, unresolved question in epigenetics.

proposals.title: Systematic identification of functional, repeat-derived protein domains in bacterial virulence and stress response systems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 genomic analyses often dismiss repetitive and intergenic sequences as non-functional 'junk DNA'. However, emerging evidence, including Aravind's work on the phage shock protein (PSP) system's evolutionary history, suggests these regions can encode novel functional elements. This proposal addresses the critical gap in systematically identifying and characterizing such elements, particularly in the context of bacterial stress and virulence systems where rapid adaptation is key.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bacterial genomes harbor a reservoir of previously unannotated, repeat-derived protein domains that have been functionally integrated into core systems governing envelope stress, virulence, and translation quality control.
proposals.method: We will leverage Aravind's expertise in comparative genomics and sensitive sequence analysis (e.g., PSI-BLAST, HMMs) to mine bacterial genomes for repetitive, low-complexity sequences associated with known virulence and stress systems (e.g., PSP, VirB). Candidate sequences will be analyzed using the GATD (Genomic Association for Tandem Duplications) method to assess their evolutionary dynamics. Predicted domains will be tested functionally in model systems (e.g., B. subtilis, E. coli) for roles in stress response, analogous to the MutS2 ribosome-splitting or PSP complex assembly mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic framework for moving beyond the 'junk DNA' paradigm in prokaryotes by cataloging a new class of functional, repeat-derived elements. It will directly expand our understanding of the genetic toolkit available for bacterial adaptation, potentially revealing novel regulatory modules or protein interaction motifs in stress response pathways.
proposals.novelty: While repeats are known to drive evolution, their direct, conserved functional roles as integrated protein domains in bacterial systems are poorly characterized. This approach uniquely combines Aravind's deep domain expertise with a focused search in under-explored genomic regions linked to his established research themes.

proposals.title: A Multi-Omics and Functional Genomics Pipeline for the Systematic Validation of Uncharacterized Bacterial and Mitochondrial Genes
proposals.motivation: Genome sequencing projects have left a vast reservoir of predicted genes, particularly in bacterial and organellar genomes, with no experimental validation of their expression or function. This gap obscures our understanding of core cellular systems, including stress responses (e.g., Phage Shock Protein), virulence mechanisms, and mitochondrial biology. A systematic approach is needed to move these computational predictions into the realm of experimentally verified biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant fraction of currently unannotated predicted genes in bacterial and mitochondrial genomes are expressed under specific conditions and encode proteins with roles in stress response, virulence, or core cellular systems, many of which may function through novel protein-protein interactions.
proposals.method: We will establish a high-throughput pipeline integrating condition-specific RNA-seq (transcriptomics) and mass spectrometry-based proteomics to confirm expression of predicted open reading frames. For genes showing expression, we will employ CRISPR-interference (CRISPRi) in model bacteria (e.g., B. subtilis) and targeted knockout/knockdown in mitochondrial systems to assess phenotypic consequences under stress and virulence-inducing conditions. Protein-protein interaction networks for validated genes will be probed using bacterial two-hybrid screens and affinity purification-mass spectrometry (AP-MS), leveraging Aravind's expertise in systems like MutS2 and PSP to identify novel functional partners.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a first-pass functional catalog for thousands of uncharacterized genes, directly linking sequence to expression and phenotype. It will identify novel components of bacterial virulence systems and mitochondrial protein networks, providing a rich resource for the study of cellular stress responses and evolution of protein domains.
proposals.novelty: While individual omics studies exist, this proposal uniquely integrates multi-omics validation with targeted functional genetics and interaction mapping specifically for genes of unknown function, applying a systems biology lens honed from the study of specific systems like PSP and MutS2 to a genome-scale problem.

proposals.title: Systematic identification of cryptic bacterial virulence promoters and enhancers through integration of evolutionary signatures and deep learning
proposals.motivation: The vast non-coding genome of bacterial pathogens harbors critical, yet poorly understood, regulatory elements that control virulence gene expression in response to host stresses. Current promoter prediction tools, often based on consensus motifs, fail to capture the diversity and context-dependent nature of these elements, especially those activated by systems like the Phage Shock Protein (PSP) response. A comprehensive, systematic approach is needed to decode this regulatory landscape.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating deep evolutionary conservation signatures (like those used in protein domain analysis) with sequence-based deep learning models will reveal a previously hidden layer of regulatory architecture, including non-canonical promoters and distal enhancer-like elements, that orchestrates bacterial virulence programs.
proposals.method: We will develop a hybrid computational pipeline. First, we will use GATD-like profile hidden Markov models to identify evolutionarily conserved, structured non-coding blocks in the intergenic regions of pathogenic bacterial pan-genomes. These conserved blocks will serve as anchors. We will then train a convolutional neural network (CNN) combined with a bidirectional long short-term memory (BiLSTM) layer on high-resolution transcription start site (TSS) and chromatin accessibility data (e.g., from prior studies) to learn local sequence syntax. The model will be validated using targeted mutagenesis and reporter assays in model systems, focusing on virulence-associated regulons.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, high-resolution map of putative regulatory elements for key virulence systems, moving beyond simple motif discovery. It will establish a generalizable framework for non-coding element discovery that combines evolutionary and sequence-syntax information. The findings will provide direct testable hypotheses for experimentalists studying bacterial pathogenesis and stress responses.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the specific application of protein domain discovery logic (profile HMMs for conservation) to the non-coding regulatory space of bacteria, coupled with state-of-the-art deep learning, to predict complex regulatory features like enhancers, which are poorly characterized in prokaryotes compared to eukaryotes.

proposals.title: A Pan-Genomic Atlas of Horizontal Gene Transfer: Mapping the Network of Prokaryotic Gene Flow and Its Impact on Virulence and Stress Systems
proposals.motivation: Horizontal gene transfer (HGT) is a primary driver of prokaryotic evolution, enabling rapid adaptation, including the spread of virulence factors and stress response systems. However, pre-2026 studies are limited in scale, typically analyzing dozens to hundreds of genomes, creating a fragmented view of gene flow. A comprehensive, pan-genome-scale analysis across thousands of prokaryotic genomes is essential to understand the global network of HGT and its role in shaping functional systems like the phage shock protein (PSP) response and virulence-associated operons.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pan-genome-scale analysis will reveal a highly structured, non-random network of HGT, where specific functional modules—particularly those involved in envelope stress (e.g., PSP), virulence, and translation rescue (e.g., MutS2-related systems)—are transferred as cohesive units across major phylogenetic boundaries, shaping the adaptive landscape of prokaryotes.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-2026 scalable methods, constructing a computational pipeline to analyze >10,000 complete prokaryotic genomes from NCBI. HGT events will be inferred using a consensus of phylogenetic incongruence (using tools like RIATA-HGT), gene composition anomalies (via pangenome decomposition with PanX), and nucleotide composition outliers. Network analysis (using graph theory libraries) will map donor-recipient relationships and identify hubs of gene exchange. Functional enrichment will be performed using pre-2026 databases (e.g., COG, TIGRFAM) to link transferred modules to systems like PSP, GATD domains, and virulence factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, functional map of HGT across the prokaryotic tree of life, quantifying the flow of specific adaptive systems. It will directly link patterns of large-scale gene flow to the evolutionary history of stress response and virulence mechanisms studied in the researcher's prior work, providing a macroevolutionary context for molecular discoveries.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the unprecedented scale of analysis—shifting from case studies on specific gene families to a systematic, pan-genomic network reconstruction—and in explicitly integrating this network with deep functional insights from bacterial stress response and protein degradation systems (e.g., PSP, MutS2, C-degron pathways).

proposals.title: Non-catalytic transcriptional regulation by TET dioxygenases: defining the protein-protein interaction network and its functional consequences
proposals.motivation: TET family 5-methylcytosine dioxygenases are central epigenetic regulators, but their proposed non-catalytic roles in transcription remain poorly defined. Understanding these mechanisms is critical for interpreting TET loss-of-function phenotypes in development and disease, which may reflect disruption of both catalytic and non-catalytic functions. This addresses a significant gap in the epigenetic toolkit, analogous to understanding non-catalytic roles of other chromatin-modifying enzymes.
proposals.hypothesis: TET proteins, particularly TET2 and TET3, regulate specific transcriptional programs through direct protein-protein interactions with chromatin complexes, and this regulation is independent of their canonical catalytic activity on DNA methylation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach: 1) Use quantitative affinity purification coupled with mass spectrometry (AP-MS) in isogenic cell lines expressing wild-type or catalytically dead (HxD mutant) TET proteins to identify stable interaction partners. 2) Integrate these interaction data with existing TET ChIP-seq and RNA-seq datasets from catalytic mutants using computational systems biology approaches, including network analysis (e.g., Cytoscape) and correlation algorithms, to pinpoint interactions linked to transcriptional changes. 3) Validate key interactions (e.g., with specific transcription factors or chromatin remodelers) via bacterial two-hybrid screening (a method in the researcher's repertoire) and co-immunoprecipitation, assessing functional impact on reporter gene expression in catalytic null backgrounds.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the first comprehensive, catalytic-activity-independent protein interaction network for mammalian TET proteins. It will establish a mechanistic framework for non-catalytic transcriptional regulation by TETs, explaining phenotypes observed in catalytic mutants and offering new paradigms for interpreting TET-related pathologies like hematological malignancies.
proposals.novelty: While TET's enzymatic function is well-studied, systematically mapping its non-catalytic interactome and directly linking specific interactions to transcriptional outputs is a novel and non-obvious extension of its biology, moving beyond the sole focus on DNA methylation oxidation.

proposals.title: Single-locus, real-time tracking of TET-mediated demethylation kinetics reveals element-specific regulatory logic
proposals.motivation: Genome-wide studies of TET enzyme activity have established its role in DNA demethylation but obscure the precise kinetics and regulatory logic at individual loci. Understanding how demethylation dynamics at specific promoters, enhancers, or transposable elements control gene expression and cellular states remains a critical gap, particularly in contexts like bacterial virulence or mitochondrial-nuclear communication where epigenetic regulation is implicated.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TET-mediated demethylation proceeds with distinct, locus-specific kinetics dictated by local chromatin architecture and trans-acting factors, and that these kinetics are a primary determinant of transcriptional timing and amplitude.
proposals.method: We will adapt and integrate CRISPR-dCas9-based imaging systems (e.g., SunTag) to tether fluorescently labeled TET catalytic domains to specific genomic loci in live mammalian cells. Demethylation kinetics will be tracked in real time using engineered, site-specific methylation biosensors based on methylation-sensitive fluorescent reporters (e.g., modified methyl-CpG binding domain fusions). Computational analysis will employ kinetic modeling frameworks, adapted from bacterial virulence gene expression systems analysis, to deconvolve rates of demethylation initiation, progression, and stabilization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for quantifying epigenetic enzyme kinetics in vivo at single-locus resolution. It will directly link demethylation dynamics to transcriptional outputs for specific gene regulatory elements, moving beyond correlative bulk observations to a causal, mechanistic understanding.
proposals.novelty: While genome-wide snapshots of methylation are common, this proposal is novel in combining precise locus targeting with real-time, quantitative tracking of demethylation kinetics in living cells, an approach not yet achieved pre-2026.

proposals.title: Identification and Mechanistic Characterization of Non-Canonical Enzymes Mediating TET-Like DNA Demethylation in Invertebrates and Plants
proposals.motivation: DNA demethylation, catalyzed by TET enzymes in animals, is crucial for epigenetic regulation, development, and disease. However, many invertebrates and plants lack clear TET homologs, presenting a major gap in understanding the conservation and evolutionary origins of active DNA demethylation pathways. Resolving this is key to a unified view of epigenetic control across eukaryotes.
proposals.hypothesis: Invertebrate and plant lineages that lack canonical TET proteins possess structurally distinct, functionally analogous enzymes that perform oxidative demethylation of 5-methylcytosine, potentially derived from alternative Fe(II)/2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase families or novel protein folds.
proposals.method: We will employ a computational strategy combining sensitive sequence profile searches (e.g., HMMER, PSI-BLAST) and structural homology detection (HHpred) to identify candidate dioxygenases in species lacking TETs. Candidate genes will be cloned and tested for 5mC oxidation activity in vitro using established LC-MS/MS assays. In vivo function will be probed via genetic knockout/complementation in model systems (e.g., C. elegans, A. thaliana mutants), assessing DNA methylation dynamics by whole-genome bisulfite sequencing and phenotypic rescue.
proposals.contribution: This work will either identify the long-sought demethylases in key eukaryotic lineages, revealing a striking case of convergent molecular evolution, or demonstrate that TET-independent mechanisms dominate, reshaping models of epigenetic evolution. It will establish a functional and evolutionary framework for DNA demethylation across the tree of life.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond cataloging TET homologs to actively searching for functional analogs using a combined computational and biochemical approach tailored to Aravind's expertise in protein evolution and enzyme mechanisms, bridging his work on bacterial systems (e.g., PSP, MutS2) to eukaryotic epigenetic puzzles.

proposals.title: Alternative Oligomeric States in AAA+ Protein Function: A Systematic Investigation of Non-Hexameric Assemblies in Bacterial Virulence and Stress Response
proposals.motivation: AAA+ proteins are universally assumed to function as hexameric rings for ATP-dependent substrate unfolding and translocation, a paradigm based on classic systems like ClpX and FtsH. However, pre-2026 studies of bacterial virulence-associated AAA+ proteins, such as VirB-like systems and the phage shock protein (PSP) interactome, hint at diverse oligomeric states and non-canonical functions. Challenging this architectural dogma is crucial for understanding the full mechanistic repertoire of this superfamily in processes like stress response and host-pathogen interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of AAA+ proteins, particularly those associated with bacterial virulence and envelope stress (e.g., PSP partners and VirB homologs), perform essential chaperone-like functions through stable non-hexameric assemblies (e.g., dimers, tetramers, or helical filaments) without forming canonical rings.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged approach combining structural biology, biochemistry, and bacterial genetics. First, we will use size-exclusion chromatography coupled to multi-angle light scattering (SEC-MALS) and native mass spectrometry to determine the native oligomeric states of candidate AAA+ proteins (e.g., from PSP and VirB systems) in vitro. Second, we will utilize cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) to visualize their architectures, with a specific focus on identifying asymmetric or helical assemblies. Third, functional assays (ATPase activity, substrate binding/unfolding) will be correlated with oligomeric state, using targeted mutations to disrupt specific interfaces. Finally, bacterial virulence or stress response phenotypes will be assessed for mutants locked in specific oligomeric forms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new functional class of AAA+ proteins operating outside the hexameric ring paradigm, directly expanding the mechanistic understanding of this superfamily. It will provide a structural and functional framework for re-interpreting the roles of numerous uncharacterized bacterial AAA+ proteins in virulence and stress, potentially revealing novel targets for antimicrobial strategies.
proposals.novelty: While previous work has focused on hexameric AAA+ machines, this proposal systematically investigates stable, functional non-hexameric assemblies, a concept only hinted at in pre-2026 literature. It uniquely bridges Aravind's expertise in bacterial virulence systems and protein evolution with cutting-edge structural methods to test a fundamental architectural assumption.

proposals.title: Systematic identification of non-canonical functional roles in the AAA+ superfamily through integrated sequence-structure-activity analysis
proposals.motivation: The AAA+ superfamily is universally defined by a conserved ATPase module and is canonically associated with chaperone-like functions in macromolecular assembly and disassembly. However, pre-2026 studies on specific members, such as bacterial MutS2 in ribosome splitting and certain viral AAA+ proteins in virulence, hint at divergent mechanistic roles. A systematic investigation is needed to determine if these are rare exceptions or indicative of a broader, underappreciated functional diversity within this fundamental protein class.
proposals.hypothesis: A significant subset of AAA+ proteins, particularly those in understudied clades or associated with stress and virulence systems, have evolved primary functions that are mechanistically distinct from canonical assembly/disassembly chaperone activity.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-tiered computational and experimental strategy. First, a sensitive sequence analysis pipeline using HMMER and CLANS clustering will define AAA+ clades, with a focus on those enriched in bacterial virulence (VirB-like) and mitochondrial systems. For candidate clades, structural modeling with AlphaFold2 and Rosetta will predict active site geometries and potential substrate interfaces. High-priority candidates will be tested biochemically using purified systems (e.g., B. subtilis homologs) for ATPase stimulation by non-canonical substrates and in vivo via genetic interaction screens (GATD-like approaches) to dissociate chaperone function from other phenotypic outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a functional map of the AAA+ superfamily that explicitly categorizes proteins by mechanistic role, not just evolutionary lineage. It will provide a framework for re-interpreting the biology of AAA+ proteins in pathogenesis and organelle function, and potentially establish new functional subclasses within this superfamily.
proposals.novelty: While previous work identifies specific AAA+ proteins with unusual functions, this proposal systematically tests the universality of the chaperone paradigm across the entire superfamily, leveraging contemporary structural predictions and focused phenotypic assays to uncover novel mechanistic themes.

proposals.title: Systematic Discovery of Divergent AAA+ ATPases in Bacterial and Organellar Systems Using Sensitive Profile HMM Searches
proposals.motivation: AAA+ ATPases are a vast superfamily of molecular machines critical for protein unfolding, membrane remodeling, and DNA replication. Current sequence-similarity searches (e.g., BLAST, PSI-BLAST) often fail to detect highly divergent members, especially in understudied bacterial lineages and organellar systems like mitochondria, which are evolutionarily derived from bacteria. This gap limits our understanding of the functional diversity and evolutionary history of these essential systems, including those linked to virulence and stress response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that curated profile Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) from databases like Pfam and TIGRFAM, when applied in a systematic, hierarchical search strategy, will reveal a significant number of previously undetected, divergent AAA+ ATPases, particularly in bacterial genomes and mitochondrial proteomes.
proposals.method: We will construct a comprehensive AAA+ HMM library from pre-2026 Pfam/TIGRFAM models (e.g., AAA, AAA_2, AAA_3, AAA_5). Using HMMER3, we will perform iterative, sensitive searches against a curated database of complete bacterial and mitochondrial proteomes. Candidate hits will be validated through secondary structure prediction, domain architecture analysis, and phylogenetic placement. The approach will be benchmarked against known AAA+ families and negative controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a significantly expanded catalog of AAA+ proteins, providing new candidates for functional studies in bacterial cell biology and mitochondrial dynamics. It will also offer insights into the deep evolutionary relationships between bacterial and organellar AAA+ systems, potentially revealing novel lineage-specific adaptations related to stress and virulence.
proposals.novelty: While HMMs are standard, their systematic, hierarchical application to uncover the 'dark matter' of the AAA+ superfamily across all bacterial and mitochondrial lineages is novel. This bridges Aravind's expertise in bacterial systems/virulence and mitochondrial evolution with a powerful, established bioinformatic method to ask a new, comprehensive question.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Structural Genomics Pipeline for the Systematic Classification and Functional Annotation of AAA+ ATPases in Bacterial Virulence Systems
proposals.motivation: AAA+ ATPases are a vast superfamily of molecular machines critical for diverse cellular processes, including protein unfolding, membrane remodeling, and DNA replication. In pathogenic bacteria, specific AAA+ proteins are often central to virulence-associated systems, such as secretion systems and toxin delivery, but many remain structurally uncharacterized, limiting mechanistic understanding. A systematic, high-throughput structural survey is needed to move beyond anecdotal studies and establish a robust classification linking AAA+ structural features to their roles in bacterial pathogenesis and cellular homeostasis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying a high-throughput structural genomics pipeline to a curated set of uncharacterized bacterial AAA+ proteins, particularly those linked to virulence operons, will reveal novel structural clades and conserved architectural principles that predict specific functional roles in cellular stress response and host-pathogen interaction.
proposals.method: We will establish a pipeline leveraging high-throughput cloning and expression screening in bacterial systems (e.g., E. coli and B. subtilis) for 50-100 target AAA+ proteins identified from genomic context analysis of virulence loci. Purified proteins will be subjected to robotic crystallization trials and single-particle cryo-EM grid preparation. Structures will be solved using fragment-based molecular replacement with known AAA+ domains and de novo model building in Phenix and Coot. Systematic classification will be performed using deep learning-based structural alignment algorithms (e.g., Dali and AlphaFold2, pre-2026 versions) and comparative analysis of inter-domain geometries and pore-loop features.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a publicly accessible atlas of novel AAA+ structures, significantly expanding the coverage of this superfamily. It will provide a refined, structure-based classification scheme that links specific architectural motifs to biological functions, such as substrate recognition or membrane association. The findings will directly inform mechanistic studies of bacterial virulence systems and related eukaryotic counterparts, like mitochondrial quality control AAA+ proteases.
proposals.novelty: While individual AAA+ structures are common, a targeted, high-throughput pipeline focused on proteins from virulence-associated bacterial systems for systematic classification is lacking. The integration of genomic context (operon structure) with deep structural comparison to predict function moves beyond sequence-based phylogeny and leverages pre-2026 structural bioinformatics at scale.

proposals.title: Predicting AAA+ Protease Substrate Specificity and Virulence Adaptor Recruitment via Coevolutionary Sequence Analysis
proposals.motivation: AAA+ proteases, such as ClpXP and FtsH, are central to protein quality control and regulation in bacteria and mitochondria, but their substrate recognition rules remain incompletely understood. This is critical for bacterial virulence systems, where AAA+ proteases often regulate key effectors, and for mitochondrial function, where their dysfunction is linked to disease. Current methods struggle to predict which specific adaptors (e.g., bacterial virulence regulators like VirB) or degrons are recognized by a given AAA+ machine.
proposals.hypothesis: Coevolutionary signals within multiple sequence alignments of AAA+ protease domains and their known adaptors/substrates encode specific interaction interfaces, allowing the prediction of novel physiological partners and specificity-determining residues.
proposals.method: We will apply Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) and related statistical methods (e.g., plmDCA) to large, curated multiple sequence alignments of AAA+ protease families (e.g., Clp, FtsH, Lon) and their known adaptor families (e.g., UmuD, RssB, mitochondrial membrane adaptors). This will be integrated with structural data (e.g., AlphaFold2 multimer predictions, pre-2026) to map co-evolving residue pairs to physical interfaces. Specificity predictions will be tested experimentally in model systems (e.g., B. subtilis, E. coli) using bacterial two-hybrid assays and degradation reporters, building on the lab's expertise with MutS2 and PSP systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a predictive map of AAA+ interaction specificity, identifying new adaptor-protease pairs relevant to bacterial stress response and virulence. It will establish a generalizable computational pipeline for inferring functional interactions in multi-protein machines and provide mechanistic insight into how AAA+ systems evolved distinct regulatory roles in bacterial and organellar contexts.
proposals.novelty: While coevolution analysis is established for protein folding and dimer interactions, its systematic application to predict the specific substrate and adaptor repertoire of large, multifunctional AAA+ machines is novel. This bridges protein biophysics (correlated mutations) with bacterial systems biology (virulence networks) and organellar proteostasis.

proposals.title: A Pan-Proteome Census of AAA+ ATPases: Evolutionary Dynamics, Functional Diversification, and Virulence-Associated Lineages
proposals.motivation: AAA+ ATPases are a vast superfamily of molecular machines critical for protein homeostasis, genome stability, and organelle function, yet their evolutionary history is fragmented, studied primarily in model organisms. A comprehensive census across all sequenced life is lacking, obscuring the full scope of their diversification, especially in understudied bacterial pathogens where they often underpin virulence systems. This project will systematically map AAA+ protein evolution to uncover novel clades, trace the origins of virulence-associated assemblies like bacterial secretion systems, and elucidate their co-evolution with cellular compartments such as mitochondria.
proposals.hypothesis: A pan-proteome analysis will reveal previously unrecognized, deeply-branching AAA+ protein lineages, identify specific clades that have been repeatedly co-opted into bacterial virulence and organelle biogenesis systems, and uncover fundamental principles governing the expansion and functional specialization of this superfamily across the tree of life.
proposals.method: We will employ a scalable computational pipeline using sensitive profile Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) built from pre-2026 AAA+ clan alignments (e.g., from Pfam/NCBI CDD) to query all publicly available proteomes (NCBI, UniProt). Initial hits will be clustered using MMseqs2 to reduce redundancy, followed by iterative sequence-profile searches (HMMER3/Jackhmmer) for remote homology detection. A massive multiple sequence alignment will be constructed (MAFFT-linsi), and phylogenies will be inferred using fast, scalable methods (IQ-TREE 2 with ModelFinder) for major clades. Functional and genomic context analysis will integrate domain architectures (using RPS-BLAST against CDD), genomic neighborhood data (for operons/phage islands), and taxonomic metadata.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first definitive catalog and evolutionary framework for the AAA+ superfamily, identifying new protein families and tracing the evolutionary pathways that led to their recruitment into complex systems like the phage shock protein (PSP) response, VirB/D4 type IV secretion systems, and mitochondrial quality control. It will generate testable predictions about the function of uncharacterized AAA+ proteins in pathogenesis and cellular stress response.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies have characterized specific AAA+ families, this project's scale and integrative phylogenomic approach across all domains of life is unprecedented. It uniquely links fundamental AAA+ protein evolution to the practical context of bacterial virulence systems and organelle biology, directly extending Aravind's expertise in these areas.

proposals.title: A Structural Grammar for P-loop NTPases: A Fold-Centric Census Reveals Hidden Functional Diversity
proposals.motivation: P-loop NTPases, one of the most ancient and functionally diverse protein superfamilies, are primarily classified by sequence, which can obscure deep evolutionary relationships and functional analogs. This is analogous to the historical limitations of vocabulary-based language classification. A systematic, structure-based census is needed to map the full functional landscape of this essential catalytic module, particularly in systems like bacterial virulence factors and mitochondrial proteins where divergent P-loops may perform conserved roles.
proposals.hypothesis: Clustering P-loop-containing domains based on the precise spatial and topological arrangement of the P-loop within its structural context—its 'structural grammar'—will reveal evolutionarily and functionally coherent groups that are missed by sequence similarity alone.
proposals.method: We will compile a comprehensive set of P-loop-containing domains from the PDB and AlphaFold2 (pre-2026 v2.3) database. Using the Dali and Foldseek algorithms for structural alignment, we will perform all-vs-all comparisons focusing on the core Rossmann-like fold. We will then apply graph-based clustering (e.g., Leiden algorithm) to the resulting structural similarity network, using metrics that emphasize the geometry and connectivity of the P-loop relative to surrounding secondary structures. Functional annotation will be cross-referenced with databases like UniProt and COG.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first high-resolution, structure-based phylogeny of the P-loop superfamily, creating a definitive census. It will predict novel functional assignments for uncharacterized members and identify structurally divergent yet functionally analogous P-loops in systems of interest, such as bacterial virulence (e.g., VirB-like ATPases) and mitochondrial complexes.
proposals.novelty: The approach shifts the unit of classification from sequence motifs to the three-dimensional structural grammar of the fold, a non-obvious move given the dominance of sequence-based hierarchies. It systematically applies this concept to the entire P-loop superfamily, moving beyond isolated case studies.

proposals.title: Uncovering the Hidden Structural Diversity of P-loop NTPases: A Fold-Centric Analysis Reveals Sequence-Divergent Clades
proposals.motivation: P-loop NTPases are a vast, evolutionarily ancient superfamily critical for diverse cellular processes, from translation to signal transduction. Current sequence-based classification systems (e.g., in Pfam, CDD) are powerful but may fail to capture deep evolutionary relationships where sequence similarity has eroded beyond detection, potentially obscuring functionally important structural lineages. Identifying these structurally conserved, sequence-divergent members is essential for a complete understanding of NTPase evolution, functional prediction, and for tracing the origins of systems like bacterial virulence (VirB) and mitochondrial protein import machinery.
proposals.hypothesis: A structure-based comparison of known P-loop folds will identify distinct, evolutionarily coherent clades whose members are too sequence-divergent to be grouped by current profile Hidden Markov Model (HMM) methods, revealing a more complex and ancient phylogenetic landscape for this superfamily.
proposals.method: We will first compile a comprehensive set of P-loop domains with experimentally determined structures from the PDB and predicted high-confidence AlphaFold2 models. Structural similarity will be systematically assessed using fold comparison algorithms (DALI, TM-align) to generate a structure-based distance matrix. This will be used for hierarchical clustering to define putative fold-centric families. These structurally defined families will then be rigorously tested against sequence-based classifications by building HMM profiles for each and querying them against sequence databases (UniRef90) to evaluate recall. Finally, sensitive sequence-profile searches (HHsearch, PSI-BLAST) and co-evolutionary analysis (using tools like GREMLIN) will be applied to trace evolutionary connections within and between the newly defined structural clades.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a revised, structure-informed map of P-loop NTPase diversity, identifying novel, deep-branching lineages. It will directly test the limits of sequence-only classification and provide a benchmark for integrating structural data into protein family databases. The findings will offer new evolutionary insights into the emergence of specific P-loop systems studied in the lab, such as bacterial virulence-associated ATPases.
proposals.novelty: While structural comparisons are standard, a systematic, large-scale fold-centric census aimed explicitly at auditing and augmenting the canonical sequence-based P-loop taxonomy has not been conducted. This approach leverages the transformative impact of accurate protein structure prediction (AlphaFold2) to challenge and refine decades-old sequence-family definitions on a proteome-wide scale.

proposals.title: A Network Decomposition of P-loop GTPase Evolution: Quantifying Vertical Inheritance and Horizontal Transfer in Prokaryotic Systems
proposals.motivation: P-loop GTPases are ubiquitous, multifunctional molecular switches involved in translation, signaling, and virulence. Their complex, patchy phylogenetic distribution across prokaryotes has long suggested extensive horizontal gene transfer (HGT), which confounds attempts to reconstruct a single, coherent species tree from these sequences. This HGT obscures the deep vertical evolutionary history of core cellular systems and the horizontal spread of adaptive traits, particularly those linked to bacterial pathogenicity.
proposals.hypothesis: The evolutionary history of P-loop GTPases can be explicitly decomposed into a conserved 'vertical core' reflecting organismal phylogeny and a network of horizontal transfers, with the latter disproportionately affecting GTPase families associated with virulence and environmental adaptation.
proposals.method: We will construct a comprehensive sequence dataset of P-loop GTPases from diverse bacterial phyla. Initial maximum-likelihood trees will be compared to a trusted species tree using methods like TreeKO to identify topological conflicts indicative of HGT. We will then apply network inference algorithms (e.g., PhyloNet, DTL reconciliation) to model these conflicts explicitly. Comparative genomic analysis of genomic context (operons, flanking genes) and sequence features (e.g., GATD domains, C-terminal tails) will be used to correlate transfer events with functional shifts.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitative map of vertical versus horizontal signals in the evolution of a major protein superfamily. It will identify which GTPase clades and bacterial lineages are most prone to HGT and link specific transfer events to the dissemination of virulence-associated systems, providing a novel network-based evolutionary framework for these critical proteins.
proposals.novelty: Instead of treating HGT as noise to be overcome for tree-building, we formally model it as a central evolutionary process, using network theory to deconvolve dual inheritance signals. This integrates Aravind's expertise in bacterial systems, virulence proteins, and domain analysis (e.g., GATD) into a unified phylogenetic framework.

proposals.title: A Phylogenetic Census-Driven Campaign to Illuminate the Structural Dark Matter of the P-loop NTPase Superclass
proposals.motivation: The P-loop NTPase superclass is one of the largest and most functionally diverse protein classes, central to information processing, signaling, and metabolism across all domains of life. Despite decades of study, dozens of computationally defined families remain structurally uncharacterized, representing a 'dark matter' of protein structure. Current structural genomics efforts often prioritize tractable or disease-linked targets, leaving the most evolutionarily divergent families unexplored and the true functional and structural diversity of this superclass incomplete.
proposals.hypothesis: Prioritizing P-loop families for structure determination based on maximum phylogenetic distance from any characterized family will maximize the discovery of novel structural folds and mechanisms per experimental effort, while providing a rigorous test of the accuracy of sequence-based evolutionary classifications.
proposals.method: We will first compile all predicted P-loop families from databases like CDD and Pfam, cross-referenced with the PDB to identify uncharacterized families. For each, we will calculate its phylogenetic distance from the nearest family with a solved structure using profile-profile alignments (e.g., HHsearch) and build maximum-likelihood trees. The resulting ranked list will guide target selection. For high-priority targets, we will use AlphaFold2 (pre-2026 state) to guide construct design and identify potential complex partners. Experimental structures will be determined using a pipeline combining high-throughput crystallography, micro-electron diffraction (MicroED) for small proteins, and single-particle cryo-EM for larger complexes, leveraging resources like the NYSBC.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first structural models for numerous 'dark' P-loop families, directly testing and refining their evolutionary classification. It will reveal novel structural elaborations and nucleotide-binding modes, potentially identifying new catalytic mechanisms or allosteric regulation strategies with broad implications for understanding cellular systems and for synthetic biology applications.
proposals.novelty: This adapts the strategic phylogenetic sampling principle of the Genomic Encyclopedia of Bacteria and Archaea (GEBA) project—a proven method for exploring biological sequence space—to the systematic exploration of protein structural space, moving beyond target selection based on convenience or disease relevance.

proposals.title: A Multi-Tiered Validation Pipeline for Novel P-loop GTPase Subfamilies: Integrating Phylogenetic Robustness, Interactome Mapping, and Domain-Swap Functional Dissection
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of genome sequencing has led to a dramatic expansion in the number of computationally predicted P-loop GTPase subfamilies, many of which may be classification artifacts rather than genuine functional units. Committing to labor-intensive biochemical characterization of all candidates is inefficient and risks misallocating resources. A rigorous, pre-experimental validation pipeline is urgently needed to prioritize the most evolutionarily and biologically distinct subfamilies for further study.
proposals.hypothesis: Genuine novel P-loop GTPase subfamilies will exhibit robust phylogenetic signal across classification algorithms and will be associated with distinct protein interaction networks and non-redundant cellular functions conferred by their unique sequence features.
proposals.method: Candidate subfamilies from large-scale scans (e.g., using HMMER, CDD) will first undergo phylogenetic robustness filtering using an ensemble of methods (Neighbor-Joining, Maximum Likelihood via RAxML, Bayesian inference) with varied sequence sampling. Surviving candidates will be tagged for proximity-dependent biotinylation (BioID or TurboID) in relevant bacterial (e.g., B. subtilis) or eukaryotic cell models to map subfamily-specific interactomes. Finally, CRISPR-Cas9 will be used to engineer domain-swap constructs, replacing divergent motifs (e.g., G4, G5 loops) in novel subfamilies with canonical versions from well-characterized GTPases, followed by phenotypic profiling (e.g., growth, stress response) and in vitro GTPase activity assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, falsifiable framework for triaging novel protein family predictions, moving the field beyond sequence-based speculation. It will deliver a high-confidence set of validated novel GTPase subfamilies with preliminary functional clues from their interactomes, directly guiding targeted mechanistic studies. The domain-swap approach will establish a direct causal link between unique sequence features and biological function.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic analysis and interactome mapping are established, their sequential integration as a pre-validation filter for computational predictions is novel. The explicit use of domain-swap constructs to test the functional necessity of divergent motifs within a putative subfamily provides a direct, engineered test of subfamily identity that goes beyond correlation.

proposals.title: Phylogenomic Reconstruction of the LUCA NTPome: A Network Analysis of Primordial Energy Transduction and Signaling
proposals.motivation: Nucleotide triphosphates (NTPs) are the universal energy currencies and substrates for information processing in all cells, but the full complement of NTP-utilizing systems in the Last Universal Common Ancestor (LUCA) remains unresolved. Existing studies focus on single superfamilies (e.g., AAA+, ABC transporters), failing to capture the integrated network of NTP-driven processes that must have underpinned early cellular life. A systematic, pan-NTPase analysis is needed to move from a list of parts to a functional model of LUCA's core metabolic and regulatory circuitry.
proposals.hypothesis: LUCA possessed a complex, synergistic 'NTPome'—a core set of P-loop, ABC, AAA+, and kinase-fold NTPases that were functionally integrated to support its energy metabolism, transport, translation, and primitive signaling, with specific families co-evolving with essential pathways like nucleotide biosynthesis and ribosome function.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-step phylogenomic pipeline: 1) Build comprehensive hidden Markov model (HMM) profiles for all major NTPase folds using sensitive sequence searches (HMMER, JackHMMER) against a curated database of prokaryotic and archaeal proteomes. 2) Reconstruct gene trees for each superfamily using maximum likelihood (IQ-TREE) and Bayesian methods, reconciling them with the species tree to infer ancestral presence/absence in LUCA. 3) Integrate these results with metabolic network reconstruction (using tools like KEGG and MetaCyc) to map NTPase functions onto core LUCA pathways, identifying functional clusters and dependencies. 4) Apply co-evolution and network analysis (e.g., using the STRING framework) to test for ancient functional associations between NTPase families and other essential systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first unified, functionally annotated catalog of NTPases in LUCA, moving beyond single-gene family studies. It will provide a mechanistic, systems-level model for how primordial life coordinated energy transduction with information processing, offering testable predictions about early cellular evolution. The resulting phylogenetic framework and HMM library will be a public resource for future studies of enzyme evolution.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, network-based approach that synthesizes phylogenomics of diverse NTPase folds with metabolic reconstruction, explicitly testing for functional synergy in LUCA, rather than treating these enzyme families as independent evolutionary units.

proposals.title: Adversarial HMM Phylogenetics: An Alignment-Free, Iterative Framework to Uncover Deep Evolutionary Histories of P-loop NTPases
proposals.motivation: Phylogenetic reconstruction of ancient protein superfamilies like P-loop NTPases is confounded by deep divergence and model bias. Standard methods, which rely on a single profile HMM built from an initial alignment, can systematically exclude or misplace novel, divergent clades discovered in metagenomic data, reinforcing existing taxonomic biases. This limits our ability to map the true evolutionary landscape of these essential enzymes, which include key virulence factors (e.g., VirB-like ATPases) and mitochondrial proteins.
proposals.hypothesis: An iterative, alignment-free clustering of diverse NTPase sequences will reveal novel, phylogenetically coherent groups that are missed by standard HMM models, and using these groups to build multiple, targeted HMMs will produce a more robust and unbiased consensus phylogeny of deep NTPase relationships.
proposals.method: We will apply alignment-free methods (e.g., k-mer composition using MMseqs2 or DIAMOND) to cluster a massive, diverse dataset of P-loop NTPases from public databases and metagenomes without an alignment guide. From these clusters, we will construct multiple, targeted profile HMMs using HMMER3. Phylogenetic trees will be inferred from each model (using IQ-TREE or FastTree) and rigorously compared for topological congruence using tools like TreeCmp. A final consensus topology will be built only from robust, model-invariant splits, explicitly quantifying and mitigating single-model bias.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, reproducible pipeline for mitigating model bias in deep phylogenetics. It will yield a significantly revised, data-driven map of P-loop NTPase evolution, likely revealing previously hidden clades and connections relevant to the origins of systems like bacterial secretion (VirB) and mitochondrial machinery. The adversarial framework itself will be a methodological advance for protein family analysis.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the standard phylogenetics workflow by using alignment-free discovery to guide model creation, rather than allowing a single initial model to constrain all subsequent analysis. It formally treats multiple HMMs as 'adversarial' models whose disagreements are analyzed to build a consensus, a strategy not commonly applied to protein family phylogenetics.

proposals.title: Deep Evolutionary Profiling of BIR Domains Reveals Ancient Structural Themes and Informs the Oncogenic Mechanism of IAP2-MALT1 Fusions
proposals.motivation: Inhibitor of Apoptosis (IAP) proteins, defined by Baculovirus IAP Repeat (BIR) domains, are critical regulators of cell death and inflammation, and their dysregulation, notably in chromosomal translocations like IAP2-MALT1, drives oncogenesis. The deep evolutionary origins and functional diversification of the BIR domain family remain poorly resolved, limiting our ability to distinguish ancient, conserved functions from recently evolved, pathological traits in cancer contexts. Pre-2026 advances in sensitive homology detection and phylogenetics, as applied to other protein systems, now enable this exploration.
proposals.hypothesis: Profile HMM-based phylogenetics will uncover deeply branching, sequence-divergent BIR homologs, revealing an ancient evolutionary map that identifies the core, conserved functional residues responsible for oncogenic activity in fusion proteins like IAP2-MALT1 as derived from primordial pro-survival mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will construct a high-sensitivity profile HMM for the BIR domain using HMMER3, iteratively searching diverse sequence databases (UniRef100, metagenomic datasets) to capture remote homologs. A maximum-likelihood phylogeny will be reconstructed from the resulting multiple sequence alignment using IQ-TREE with model selection, visualizing deep branches and clade-specific conservation. Evolutionary trace analysis will map conserved residues onto BIR domain structures to predict functional sites and potential interaction interfaces relevant to the IAP2-MALT1 fusion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution, deep evolutionary history of the BIR domain family, identifying novel homologs and major diversification events. It will directly link evolutionary conservation to molecular mechanism in cancer, generating testable predictions about critical functional residues in oncogenic fusions and offering a phylogenetic framework for interpreting IAP biology across eukaryotes and viruses.
proposals.novelty: Applying metagenomics-style profile HMM phylogenetics, typically used for discovery in microbial systems, to the cancer-relevant BIR domain family is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond standard BLAST-based phylogenies to connect deep evolutionary discovery with specific mechanistic hypotheses in structural oncology.

proposals.title: A Dual-Stream Evolution-Structure Network for Remote Detection of P-loop NTPase Homologs
proposals.motivation: The P-loop NTPase fold is a ubiquitous and functionally diverse catalytic module in proteins involved in essential processes like signaling, transport, and virulence. Current sequence-based methods, including protein language models (pLMs), often fail to detect remote homologs where sequence similarity is low but the core structural and biophysical architecture of the P-loop is conserved. This gap limits our ability to fully annotate genomes and understand the deep evolutionary history of this critical functional motif.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid neural network that jointly learns from deep evolutionary sequence embeddings and predicted 3D structural features will significantly outperform purely sequence-based models in detecting remote P-loop homologs, revealing previously hidden evolutionary relationships.
proposals.method: We will construct a dual-stream neural network. The sequence stream will use embeddings from a pre-trained pLM (e.g., ESM-2). The structure stream will process features (e.g., backbone torsion angles, residue contact maps, electrostatic potential patches) predicted from sequence by AlphaFold2 or RoseTTAFold. These streams will be integrated via a cross-attention mechanism. The network will be trained on a curated dataset of confirmed P-loop proteins and negative controls, using a contrastive or binary classification loss to learn a robust joint representation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new computational tool for sensitive remote homology detection of a fundamental protein fold. It will provide a framework for integrating evolutionary and structural information that can be generalized to other motifs. The analysis may uncover novel, distantly related P-loop proteins in bacterial virulence systems and mitochondrial machineries, areas of direct relevance to the researcher's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While pLMs capture evolutionary constraints and structure prediction is mature, the explicit, joint training of a network on both information streams for a specific, functionally defined motif (rather than general fold classification) is a novel integration. This moves beyond using structure as mere post-prediction validation to making it a core, co-equal input for detection.

proposals.title: Semantic Homology Search: A pLM-Embedding and Approximate Nearest Neighbor Framework for Uncovering Distant Evolutionary Relationships in P-loop Proteins
proposals.motivation: Detecting remote homology is crucial for inferring protein function and evolutionary history, but sequence-based methods like PSI-BLAST fail at extreme divergence. This gap obscures deep evolutionary connections, such as those between bacterial virulence (VirB) systems and core cellular machines like mitochondrial translocases. Protein language models (pLMs) pre-trained on massive sequence datasets have learned rich representations of structural and functional constraints, offering a new paradigm for homology detection.
proposals.hypothesis: Dense, contextual embeddings from deep protein language models encode evolutionary relationships more sensitively than sequence similarity, enabling a semantic search framework that can identify remote homologs—particularly among P-loop NTPases—missed by profile-based methods.
proposals.method: We will generate embeddings for all sequences in UniRef100 using the ESM-2 model. For a query set of P-loop proteins (e.g., VirB11-like ATPases), we will compute their embeddings and perform efficient k-nearest neighbor searches against the pre-computed database using the HNSW graph algorithm via the FAISS library. Candidate remote homologs will be validated through rigorous phylogenetic analysis, structural comparison (via AlphaFold2 models), and examination of conserved sequence motifs and genomic context.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a scalable, open-source tool for remote homology detection that moves beyond sequence similarity. It will provide a novel, high-sensitivity map of evolutionary relationships within the vast P-loop NTPase superfamily, potentially revealing new connections between prokaryotic virulence systems and fundamental eukaryotic cellular machinery.
proposals.novelty: While pLMs are used for structure prediction and embedding, their application as a semantic search engine for systematic remote homology detection, specifically leveraging efficient ANN algorithms for large-scale database queries, is a novel and untested methodology.

proposals.title: Structure-Aware Deep Learning Uncovers Cryptic Paracaspase-Bcl10 Interaction Modules and Divergent Pathogenic Signaling Assemblies
proposals.motivation: The paracaspase MALT1 and its adaptor Bcl10 form a critical signaling hub in the CBM complex, driving NF-κB activation in lymphoma and autoimmunity. Current homology searches, reliant on sequence alone, likely miss highly divergent homologs of their interaction domains, obscuring the full evolutionary landscape and potential for alternative pathogenic assemblies. A systematic, structure-informed search is needed to reveal cryptic interaction modules that could rewire signaling in disease.
proposals.hypothesis: Remote, sequence-divergent homologs of the paracaspase prodomain and Bcl10 exist across eukaryotic proteomes and retain the structural determinants to form non-canonical heterodimeric interfaces, potentially nucleating alternative pathogenic signaling complexes.
proposals.method: We will employ the ESM-2 protein language model and the Foldseek structural alignment tool to perform a sensitive, embedding-based search for remote homologs of the paracaspase prodomain and Bcl10 across diverse proteomes. Identified candidates will be subjected to AlphaFold2-Multimer for high-confidence modeling of their heterodimeric interfaces with canonical partners. Interface conservation and energetic analysis (using tools like FoldX) will prioritize candidates for experimental testing of complex formation and signaling activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first atlas of predicted non-canonical paracaspase-Bcl10 interaction modules, providing testable hypotheses for novel signaling assemblies in pathology. It will establish a generalizable deep learning pipeline for the discovery of cryptic, functionally relevant protein interaction networks, bridging computational genomics and mechanistic structural biology.
proposals.novelty: Moving beyond detecting single remote homologs to predicting entire, functionally divergent protein interaction modules via a combined language model and structure-prediction approach represents a significant advance over traditional sequence-profile methods used pre-2026.

proposals.title: A Geometric Morphometric Phylogeny of the P-loop GTPase Superfamily Derived from AlphaFold2 Structural Landmarks
proposals.motivation: The P-loop GTPase superfamily is a paradigm of ancient evolutionary radiation, with members governing processes from translation to signal transduction. Current classifications, based on sequence similarity, often fail to resolve deep evolutionary relationships and can obscure structural convergences. The release of the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database provides an unprecedented, uniform corpus of predicted 3D structures, enabling a novel, morphology-first approach to phylogeny, analogous to methods used in paleontology.
proposals.hypothesis: Quantitative geometric morphometrics applied to the conserved core of AlphaFold-predicted GTPase structures will generate a phylogeny that reveals clades and evolutionary relationships orthogonal to sequence-based trees, uncovering deep homologies and functional convergences within the fold.
proposals.method: We will curate a comprehensive set of P-loop GTPase sequences and retrieve their predicted structures from the AlphaFold DB. Using established geometric morphometric software (e.g., MorphoJ), we will define 3D landmarks on the universal G-domain core, focusing on the spatial configuration of the P-loop, switch I/II regions, and nucleotide-binding pocket. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) of Procrustes-aligned landmark coordinates will yield structural feature vectors. These vectors will be clustered using neighbor-joining and maximum-likelihood algorithms to construct a purely structural phylogeny, which will be compared to sequence-based trees.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first large-scale, quantitative structural taxonomy of the GTPase superfamily, identifying potential new structural sub-families and clarifying the evolutionary trajectory of functional modules like the G-domain. It will establish a proof-of-concept for using predicted structural morphometrics as a primary tool for protein classification, complementing sequence analysis.
proposals.novelty: While AlphaFold has been used for structural inspection, its systematic application as a source of quantitative morphological data for phylogenetic clustering is novel. This approach directly transplants a paleontological solution—using shape in the absence of genetic data—to a major problem in protein evolution.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Compressed Sensing for Sparse Coevolutionary Signal Extraction in Bacterial Virulence and Stress Response Proteins
proposals.motivation: Identifying coevolving residues is crucial for predicting functional sites in proteins, but methods like Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) require large, diverse sequence alignments and can be computationally intensive. For protein families with limited sequence data, such as specific bacterial virulence factors or stress response systems like the Phage Shock Protein (PSP) complex, extracting a clear signal from noisy evolutionary data remains a challenge. A more efficient method to pinpoint the sparse network of functionally critical residues from smaller datasets would accelerate the functional annotation of these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By framing the problem of identifying direct evolutionary couplings as a sparse signal recovery task, we hypothesize that applying L1-regularized regression (LASSO) to the sequence covariance matrix, informed by phylogenetic correction, will isolate a more precise and functionally relevant set of coevolving residues than standard DCA, especially in alignments of limited depth.
proposals.method: We will implement a phylogenetic compressed sensing pipeline. First, we will generate curated multiple sequence alignments for target protein families (e.g., VirB T4SS components, PSP interactors, MutS2 domains). The inverse covariance matrix of residue pairs, representing direct couplings, will be estimated using a Graphical LASSO framework, imposing sparsity via L1 regularization. Phylogenetic non-independence will be accounted for by incorporating sequence weights derived from a phylogenetic tree or using a phylogenetic correction on the covariance matrix itself. The resulting sparse network of couplings will be validated against known functional sites from structural data and mutagenesis studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new computational tool for functional site prediction that is robust to smaller, phylogenetically biased sequence alignments common in bacterial systems biology. It will generate testable predictions for critical residues in understudied systems like the PSP envelope stress response and MutS2's ribosome-splitting activity, bridging sequence analysis with mechanistic hypotheses.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit adaptation of compressed sensing principles—specifically sparse signal recovery from underdetermined systems—to the phylogenetic coevolution problem, moving beyond the dense coupling estimates of mean-field or pseudo-likelihood DCA methods. This formalizes the inherent sparsity of functional constraints in a rigorous optimization framework.

proposals.title: A Unified Classification of NTPase Folds Based on Conserved Catalytic Coordination Geometry
proposals.motivation: NTPases are a functionally diverse superclass of enzymes critical for cellular energy transduction, signaling, and motility. Current classifications, based on sequence and fold similarity, are fragmented across distinct superfamilies (e.g., P-loop, AAA+, ABC, kinases), obscuring deep evolutionary relationships and limiting functional prediction for uncharacterized members. A unified, mechanistic framework is needed to transcend these traditional boundaries and reveal the fundamental principles underlying all NTP catalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The precise three-dimensional geometry and electrostatic properties of the catalytic metal ion (Mg2+) coordination sphere constitute a conserved, orderable 'signature' that is more fundamental than fold and can serve as a unifying principle for classifying all NTPase lineages.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-scale computational approach. First, we will curate a comprehensive structural dataset of NTPase domains from the PDB, spanning all major superfamilies. Using quantum chemical calculations (DFT) and molecular dynamics simulations, we will parameterize the coordination geometry, bond lengths/angles, and electrostatic potential of the active site. We will then apply unsupervised manifold learning and clustering algorithms (e.g., UMAP, HDBSCAN) to this high-dimensional geometric and electrostatic data to identify natural groupings and periodic trends, constructing the 'Periodic Table' framework. This will be validated by predicting and testing the nucleotide specificity of uncharacterized G-proteins and viral NTPases.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first mechanistic, fold-agnostic classification system for NTPases, analogous to the Periodic Table. It will provide a predictive map linking catalytic geometry to function, reveal novel evolutionary connections between disparate superfamilies, and establish a rigorous foundation for the engineering of synthetic NTPases with tailored activities.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond sequence and fold homology to a first-principles, physics-based ordering principle. It posits that catalytic metal coordination, not overall protein architecture, is the primary conserved and classifiable feature uniting all NTPases, a hypothesis not systematically explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Energetic Drivers of Pathogenic Signalosomes: A Pan-NTPase Classification and Structural Dissection of the Bcl10-MALT1 Paracaspase Interface
proposals.motivation: Pathogenic signaling assemblies, like the CBM (CARD11-Bcl10-MALT1) complex central to NF-κB activation in lymphoma and autoimmunity, are critical disease drivers, yet their assembly dynamics remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 structural studies of the Bcl10-MALT1 interface provide static snapshots but lack a mechanistic framework for the energy-dependent regulation of oligomerization and signal amplification. A systematic classification of NTPase folds, which are ubiquitous engines of macromolecular assembly and remodeling, has not been applied to this pathogenic signaling context.
proposals.hypothesis: The Bcl10-MALT1 interface and its higher-order assemblies are governed by a specific, catalytically active or regulatory NTPase fold, which provides the energetic driving force for signalosome formation and determines its regulatory logic.
proposals.method: We will first apply sensitive profile-profile homology detection (using HMMER and HHpred) and structure prediction (AlphaFold2) to perform a pan-NTPase fold scan of Bcl10, MALT1, and their complex, mapping them against a comprehensive pre-2026 NTPase superfamily classification (AAA+, G-proteins, kinases, etc.). Guided by this, we will use cryo-EM to determine structures of reconstituted human Bcl10-MALT1 assemblies in apo and nucleotide-bound states. Mutagenesis of predicted catalytic motifs will be coupled with in vitro NF-κB luciferase reporter assays in lymphoid cells to test functional necessity.
proposals.contribution: This work will resolve the long-standing question of whether a specific NTPase mechanism drives CBM signalosome assembly, providing a dynamic, energetic model beyond static interfaces. It will establish a generalizable pipeline—from computational fold classification to targeted structural and functional validation—to identify the 'energetic drivers' of other disease-relevant signaling complexes, linking protein fold evolution directly to pathogenic mechanism.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of large-scale, evolutionary-informed NTPase fold classification with targeted structural biology of a specific disease complex is a novel strategy to uncover hidden regulatory mechanisms, moving beyond descriptive interaction mapping to identify the catalytic core governing signal transduction.

proposals.title: A Unified Evolutionary and Functional Classification of NTPase Folds Across the Tree of Life
proposals.motivation: NTPases are fundamental to cellular energy transduction, signaling, and macromolecular remodeling, but existing classifications (e.g., for P-loop, ABC, AAA+, kinases) are largely siloed. A pan-NTPase classification is needed to systematically map the deep evolutionary relationships and functional diversification of these enzymes across all domains of life, which remains a major gap in protein evolution and systems biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sensitive, structure-based comparative genomics approach will reveal a comprehensive network of evolutionary relationships among all major NTPase folds, uncovering shared ancestral structural modules and lineage-specific functional innovations.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-tiered computational pipeline. First, we will use sensitive sequence profile-profile comparisons (HHpred) and structural alignment (DALI, TM-align) to build a similarity network of NTPase domains from major folds (P-loop, ABC, AAA+, ASKHA, RecA-like, etc.). Second, we will apply graph-based clustering and phylogenetic analysis to define superclades and infer deep evolutionary events. Third, leveraging Dr. Aravind's expertise in bacterial systems and virulence-associated proteins (e.g., VirB, PSP), we will perform focused contextual analysis on specific clades to link evolutionary history with functional systems like secretion or stress response.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first unified, publicly accessible map of NTPase fold relationships, providing a framework for predicting functions of uncharacterized NTPases. It will also generate specific, testable predictions about the evolutionary trajectory of key systems, such as the recruitment of specific AAA+ or P-loop NTPases into bacterial virulence apparatuses.
proposals.novelty: While individual NTPase classes are well-studied, a systematic, structure-based integration across all folds is unprecedented. The novelty lies in scaling classification to the entire NTPase functional super-kingdom to uncover principles of fold diversification that are invisible in isolated analyses.

proposals.title: Deconstructing the GTPase Switch: A Synthetic Biology-Inspired Design for a Minimal Functional G-Domain Circuit
proposals.motivation: P-loop GTPases are ubiquitous molecular switches in biology, but their canonical architecture is complex, often exceeding 200 residues. Understanding the minimal determinants of GTP binding, hydrolysis, and regulated switching is a fundamental problem in protein evolution and design. A synthetic biology approach, which distills complex functions into minimal genetic circuits, provides a powerful conceptual framework to address this, moving beyond traditional domain truncation studies.
proposals.hypothesis: The core switching logic of a GTPase can be reconstituted as a minimal functional circuit comprising a catalytic G-domain, essential switch elements, and a high-affinity GAP-binding motif, integrated into a de novo polypeptide scaffold.
proposals.method: We will computationally design minimal GTPase circuits using Rosetta-based protocols. Starting from canonical G-domain cores (e.g., from Arf or Ras), we will graft minimal, pre-2026 validated GAP-binding motifs (e.g., from the Arf1-ArfGAP interaction) via ultra-short, rigid linkers or by embedding them into de novo helical bundle scaffolds. Designs will be screened for structural integrity and predicted binding energies. Top designs will be experimentally validated for GTP binding (mant-GTP fluorescence), basal hydrolysis (malachite green assay), and GAP-stimulated activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-principle for a circuit-based design paradigm in enzymology, generating the smallest known functional GTPase switches. It will provide a tractable model system to dissect the energetic and structural requirements of GTPase regulation, with implications for understanding the evolution of molecular switches and engineering novel biosensors.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in treating a GTPase as a decomposable functional circuit rather than an indivisible fold, and in the explicit integration of a defined regulatory input (GAP motif) into a minimal design, a step beyond creating merely catalytically active G-domain fragments.
