proposals.title: Context-aware de novo design of synthetic GTPase regulators for a target organism
proposals.motivation: De novo protein design has advanced to create novel folds and functions, but designed proteins often fail in vivo due to poor integration with the host proteome, leading to misfolding, mislocalization, or disruptive interactions. This is particularly critical for GTPase regulators, which function within dense, specific interaction networks. A method to design proteins pre-validated for a specific cellular context would bridge the gap between computational design and functional utility in synthetic biology and therapeutic development.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a comprehensive, organism-specific GTPase interaction network as a functional context into a deep learning-based inverse design pipeline will enable the generation of novel GAP/GEF protein sequences that are predicted to be compatible with the target organism's proteomic environment, thereby increasing their functional success in vivo.
proposals.method: We will first construct a high-confidence interaction map for all P-loop GTPases in Bacillus subtilis, leveraging Aravind lab expertise in bacterial systems and protein evolution. This map, incorporating structural, localization, and genetic interaction data, will be encoded as a context graph. We will then integrate this graph as a conditioning layer into an inverse design model, such as a graph-conditioned ProteinMPNN or a fine-tuned protein language model (e.g., ESM-2). The model will be trained to generate sequences for desired regulatory functions (e.g., GAP for a specific GTPase) while minimizing predicted disruptive interactions within the host network.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational framework for context-aware protein design and a set of de novo GTPase regulator sequences for B. subtilis, experimentally testing their function and integration. It will establish a generalizable paradigm for designing synthetic proteins that are 'pre-adapted' to a host organism, significantly advancing the field of functional synthetic biology.
proposals.novelty: While network-aware drug design exists, applying a comprehensive, single-organism functional interaction network as a direct constraint for de novo protein sequence generation is a novel approach. It moves beyond designing for isolated biochemical activity to designing for seamless cellular integration.

proposals.title: Universal genomic determinants of bacterial obligate intracellular parasitism revealed by a comprehensive pan-genome analysis
proposals.motivation: Obligate intracellular bacterial pathogens (e.g., *Chlamydia*, *Rickettsia*, *Coxiella*) represent a major public health burden and a unique evolutionary paradigm, having undergone extreme genome reduction and adaptation to a parasitic lifestyle. While individual systems have been studied, a unified, pan-genomic perspective is lacking, hindering the identification of core principles governing host-dependency and virulence. A systematic comparison across all known obligate intracellular lineages is needed to distinguish lineage-specific adaptations from universal parasitism genes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core set of genes, beyond those for essential metabolism, is universally conserved across all obligate intracellular bacterial lineages, encoding functions critical for host cell entry, nutrient acquisition, and evasion of host defenses, thereby defining a minimal genetic blueprint for this lifestyle.
proposals.method: We will compile a curated dataset of high-quality genomes spanning all major obligate intracellular bacterial genera (e.g., *Chlamydiota*, *Rickettsiales*, *Legionella*-like *Coxiella*). Using established pan-genome analysis pipelines (e.g., Roary, Panaroo), we will compute the core genome (genes present in ≥95% of genomes) and accessory genome. Functional annotation will leverage sensitive homology detection (HHpred, HMMER) and structural prediction (AlphaFold2) to assign roles. We will specifically search for conserved, degenerate virulence-associated systems (VirB/D4 T4SS homologs) and stress response modules (e.g., Phage Shock Protein (PSP) system components), integrating Aravind's expertise in these domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first definitive catalog of universally conserved genes underpinning the obligate intracellular lifestyle, moving beyond single-genome studies. It will identify novel, minimal virulence determinants and stress management systems that could serve as broad-spectrum therapeutic targets. Furthermore, it will provide an evolutionary framework to understand the convergent genomic trajectories of host adaptation.
proposals.novelty: Previous studies have focused on single genera or limited comparisons; this is the first attempt to perform a truly comprehensive, all-lineage pan-genome analysis to extract universal principles. The integration of deep homology detection and structural prediction with pan-genomics, guided by specific hypotheses on systems like VirB and PSP, moves beyond standard genomic surveys to mechanistic insight.

proposals.title: Systematic identification of cryptic metabolic pathways in bacteria with streamlined genomes through integration of genomic context and activity-based protein profiling
proposals.motivation: Bacteria with reduced, streamlined metabolisms, such as obligate intracellular pathogens or endosymbionts, often encode a high proportion of hypothetical proteins. Pre-2026 work, including studies on virulence-associated systems and stress responses, suggests these uncharacterized proteins may constitute a reservoir of novel enzymatic activities that support survival in niche environments. Identifying these cryptic pathways is crucial for understanding the metabolic adaptations and host-interaction strategies of these organisms.
proposals.hypothesis: Hypothetical proteins in bacteria with streamlined genomes encode novel, lineage-specific enzymatic activities that constitute cryptic metabolic pathways, which can be revealed by integrating genomic context analysis with functional proteomic screening.
proposals.method: We will employ a dual computational and experimental pipeline. First, using genomic context analysis (e.g., gene neighborhood, operon structure, and co-evolution via mirrortree) and sensitive sequence-profile searches (HHpred, PSI-BLAST), we will prioritize candidate hypothetical proteins from model streamlined bacteria (e.g., *Chlamydia*, *Rickettsia*). Second, we will apply activity-based protein profiling (ABPP) using broad-spectrum chemical probes for enzyme classes (e.g., hydrolases, transferases) to capture their biochemical activities in bacterial lysates. Hits will be validated via recombinant expression and in vitro biochemical assays to map substrates and products, reconstructing putative pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will directly assign biochemical functions to numerous 'orphan' hypothetical proteins, revealing previously unrecognized metabolic capabilities in minimal genomes. It will establish a generalizable framework for discovering cryptic pathways, advancing our understanding of metabolic adaptation and gene loss in host-associated bacteria.
proposals.novelty: While genomic reduction is well-studied, the systematic experimental unmasking of its cryptic biochemical repertoire is not. This approach moves beyond homology-based inference to empirically discover novel enzyme chemistry encoded by genes with no known homologs, a frontier in functional genomics pre-2026.

proposals.title: Targeting Cryptosporidium parvum through bacterial-like C-degron pathway enzymes: A computational and biochemical screen for selective antiparasitic compounds
proposals.motivation: Cryptosporidium parvum is a major cause of severe diarrheal disease in children and immunocompromised individuals, with no effective drug treatment. Its genome reveals extensive horizontal gene transfer from bacteria, providing a unique therapeutic opportunity. Enzymes derived from such transfers, particularly those absent in the mammalian host, represent promising, selective drug targets.
proposals.hypothesis: C. parvum encodes bacterial/plant-like C-degron recognition enzymes, such as E3 ligases or adaptors analogous to Pirh2/CRL2-KLHDC10, which are essential for parasite proteostasis and can be selectively inhibited by compounds that do not affect the human proteolytic machinery.
proposals.method: We will first perform a sensitive homology search using HMMER and PSI-BLAST against the C. parvum proteome, using bacterial and plant C-degron pathway components (e.g., KLHDC10, other CTLH complex subunits) as queries. Identified candidates will be structurally modeled using AlphaFold2 and Rosetta to characterize putative substrate-binding pockets. High-throughput virtual screening of pre-2026 compound libraries (e.g., ZINC15) against these unique pockets will be conducted using Glide docking, followed by biochemical validation of top hits using recombinant C. parvum enzyme activity assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will identify and validate novel, parasite-specific drug targets within the C. parvum proteostasis network. It will deliver a prioritized list of small-molecule inhibitors with potential for further development as anti-cryptosporidial leads, directly addressing a critical unmet medical need.
proposals.novelty: While bacterial enzyme targeting in parasites is known, applying the recently elucidated principles of C-degron recognition—a system Aravind has studied in eukaryotes—to exploit bacterial-like proteolytic pathways in C. parvum is a novel strategy. This combines evolutionary genomics with degron biology for antiparasitic drug discovery.

proposals.title: Functional and Evolutionary Characterization of Novel Secreted Virulence-Associated Protein (SVP) Families in Bacterial Pathogens
proposals.motivation: Comparative genomics has revealed numerous uncharacterized gene families encoding predicted secreted or cell-surface proteins, often adjacent to known virulence factors in pathogen genomes. Their specific roles in host-pathogen interactions, immune evasion, or niche adaptation remain unknown, representing a critical gap in understanding pathogenesis. Systematic characterization of these proteins is essential for deciphering novel virulence mechanisms and identifying potential therapeutic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subset of these novel secreted protein families are bona fide virulence factors that directly mediate host cell attachment, manipulation, or immune modulation, and that their evolutionary history reveals patterns of horizontal gene transfer and adaptation specific to pathogenic lineages.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-pronged computational and experimental pipeline. First, using sensitive profile-profile comparisons (HHpred, HMMER) and structural prediction (AlphaFold2), we will define domain architectures and infer potential molecular functions for candidate families. Second, leveraging the GATD (Genomic Association Tandem Domain) method, we will analyze genomic context for conserved operonic associations with known virulence systems. Third, we will test functional roles in model systems (e.g., B. subtilis for secretion, cell culture for cytotoxicity) by constructing deletion mutants and assessing phenotypes related to stress response, adhesion, and host cell death.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide functional annotations for currently 'hypothetical' proteins, directly linking specific gene families to virulence mechanisms. It will establish a framework for prioritizing secreted candidates from genomic data. The findings may reveal new protein-based interactions at the host-pathogen interface, with implications for understanding infection biology.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates deep evolutionary analysis (inspired by Aravind's work on PSP and C-degron origins) with systematic functional testing of secreted proteins identified via genomic context, moving beyond sequence similarity to define function through association and structure. It applies the GATD method, previously used for domain discovery, to the specific problem of virulence factor prediction.

proposals.title: Structural and Functional Annotation of Novel Bacterial Secreted and Surface Proteins Using Deep Learning and Comparative Genomics
proposals.motivation: Bacterial pathogenesis and symbiosis are heavily mediated by secreted and surface-exposed proteins, yet many of these proteins remain uncharacterized due to a lack of recognizable domains in sequence databases. This 'dark proteome' of bacterial surfaces represents a critical gap in understanding host-microbe interactions, virulence mechanisms, and potential therapeutic targets. Pre-2026 advances in deep learning-based protein structure prediction, exemplified by AlphaFold2 and RoseTTAFold, now enable systematic exploration of these proteins' folds and potential functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-throughput structural prediction, integrated with genomic context analysis and biophysical feature prediction, will reveal novel, conserved structural domains and functional mechanisms within currently unannotated bacterial surface and secreted protein families.
proposals.method: We will curate a target set of novel, domain-less secreted/surface proteins from bacterial genomes, focusing on systems associated with virulence (e.g., T6SS, T4SS) and stress response (e.g., Phage Shock Protein interactors). We will employ AlphaFold2 and RoseTTAFold for high-throughput tertiary structure prediction. Predicted structures will be clustered using fold comparison tools (e.g., Foldseek, TM-align) to identify recurrent novel folds. These structural families will be analyzed in genomic context using tools for operon and gene neighborhood analysis (as used in studies of PSP and MutS2), and features like transmembrane helices and signal peptides will be predicted. Candidate novel domains will be experimentally validated for protein-protein interaction using bacterial two-hybrid systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first structural atlas of several novel bacterial surface protein families, moving them from sequence-only entities to structurally defined functional units. It will generate testable hypotheses about their roles in bacterial envelope stress, virulence, and interbacterial competition, directly extending Aravind's work on PSP and MutS2. The resulting structural families will populate public databases, aiding future annotation and drug discovery efforts.
proposals.novelty: While AlphaFold2 has been applied to well-defined families, its systematic use to de-orphan the vast pool of domain-less bacterial surface proteins, integrated with contextual genomic analysis inspired by the researcher's prior work on evolutionary systems biology, represents a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond mere prediction to generate functional and evolutionary insights for the most cryptic components of bacterial proteomes.

proposals.title: Quantitative Proteomic Profiling of the Graded Oxidative Stress Response in Deinococcus radiodurans Reveals Novel Regulators and Pathway Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Deinococcus radiodurans exhibits extraordinary resistance to oxidative stress, a key factor in its extreme radioresistance. While its genome encodes numerous predicted oxidative stress defense proteins, the systems-level coordination and quantitative expression dynamics of these pathways under precisely controlled stress conditions remain poorly characterized. Validating genomic predictions with proteomic data is crucial for understanding the mechanistic basis of its resilience, with implications for biotechnology and astrobiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that D. radiodurans employs a tiered, dose-dependent proteomic response to oxidative stress, where core antioxidant systems are constitutively high, and novel, uncharacterized proteins are differentially recruited at specific stress thresholds, revealing previously unrecognized regulatory networks.
proposals.method: We will subject D. radiodurans to graded doses of hydrogen peroxide and paraquat, capturing early and sustained response timepoints. Proteins will be extracted, digested, and labeled using TMT multiplexing for high-throughput quantitative comparison. Liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) data will be acquired on an Orbitrap Fusion Lumos platform. Bioinformatic analysis will utilize the MaxQuant/Andromeda pipeline for identification and quantification, followed by differential expression analysis with Perseus and functional enrichment via STRING and custom comparative genomics tools to map proteins to predicted stress regulons.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative map of protein expression dynamics across a gradient of oxidative stress in D. radiodurans. It will directly validate or refute genomic predictions of stress-related operons and likely identify novel participants in the oxidative stress response. The dataset will serve as a foundational resource for constructing accurate models of extreme stress resilience.
proposals.novelty: Unlike previous studies using single stress levels or transcriptomics, this proposal uses a graded stress proteomics approach to dissect threshold-based pathway activation, directly linking genomic predictions to protein abundance. The integration of quantitative proteomics with Aravind's expertise in comparative genomics and bacterial systems (e.g., PSP response, virulence factors) allows for the discovery of non-obvious, evolutionarily conserved stress management modules.

proposals.title: Structural and Mechanistic Elucidation of Deinococcus radiodurans MutS2 and Novel UvrD-like Helicases in Ribosome-Associated DNA Repair
proposals.motivation: Deinococcus radiodurans possesses extraordinary resistance to ionizing radiation and desiccation, implicating unique DNA repair pathways. Pre-2026 work, including Aravind's studies on B. subtilis MutS2 splitting stalled ribosomes and on bacterial virulence systems, suggests that specialized enzymes may operate at the nexus of translation and DNA repair to resolve transcription-replication conflicts and genomic damage in extreme conditions. Defining the structural basis of these activities is critical for understanding extreme stress survival.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that D. radiodurans MutS2 and a novel, lineage-specific UvrD-like helicase (DR_XXXX) form a complex that recognizes and processes stalled ribosomes on damaged DNA, facilitating repair through a mechanism distinct from canonical mismatch repair or helicase functions.
proposals.method: We will apply high-throughput X-ray crystallography. Target proteins (MutS2, DR_XXXX) will be expressed and purified using affinity and size-exclusion chromatography. Crystallization will be pursued via robotic screening. Diffraction data will be collected at synchrotron facilities and structures solved by molecular replacement using known MutS (e.g., Thermus aquaticus MutS2) and helicase domains as search models, or by experimental phasing if necessary. Iterative model building and refinement will use PHENIX and Coot. Functional assays will validate interactions and activity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution structures of key D. radiodurans repair enzymes in this functional context, revealing novel protein-protein interfaces and active site architectures. It will establish a mechanistic model for ribosome disassembly during DNA repair, bridging Aravind's expertise in bacterial stress systems and protein evolution.
proposals.novelty: While MutS2's role in ribosome splitting is known, its structural adaptation for DNA damage response in extremophiles is unexplored. Coupling this with a novel helicase partner moves beyond the study of isolated components to a systems-level understanding of a coordinated repair complex.

proposals.title: A Systems Biology Model of the Integrated DNA Damage Response Network: From Genomic Inventory to Dynamic Simulation
proposals.motivation: DNA repair is not a set of isolated pathways but a complex, integrated network with crosstalk to transcription, translation, and stress responses. Pre-2026, while inventories of repair genes exist, a predictive, dynamic model of the entire network, grounded in complete genome data and incorporating non-canonical players like MutS2, is lacking. Such a model is crucial for understanding cellular decision-making during genotoxic stress and its links to disease and bacterial virulence.
proposals.hypothesis: The DNA damage response can be accurately modeled as a condition-specific, dynamic network whose topology and emergent properties are dictated by the genomic repertoire of repair, signaling, and ancillary factors, revealing critical control nodes and system-level fragility.
proposals.method: We will construct a genome-scale, logic-based (Boolean) network model using the complete genome data of *B. subtilis* and *E. coli*. Nodes will be defined from comprehensive genomic annotation of repair (e.g., MutS2), stress response (e.g., PSP), and degradation systems (e.g., C-degron pathways). Interaction rules will be derived from literature and protein-protein interaction databases. The model will be simulated using tools like GINsim or CellNOpt to predict network states under various damage inputs. We will specifically test the model's prediction of MutS2's role in coordinating ribosome stalling with repair.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dynamic, systems-level model of the bacterial DNA damage response that integrates core repair with translation-associated and envelope stress pathways. It will generate testable predictions about network control points and the functional consequences of perturbing non-canonical players like MutS2, bridging Aravind's work on protein evolution with systems-level cellular function.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in moving beyond static pathway maps to a condition-aware, executable model that explicitly incorporates Aravind's recent findings on MutS2's ribosome-splitting and C-degron recognition into the core DNA repair logic, creating a unified framework for disparate protein systems.

proposals.title: Comparative Pan-Genomics of Extremophiles Reveals Shared and Lineage-Specific Stress Resistance Systems
proposals.motivation: Extremophiles like Deinococcus, Thermus, and Sulfolobus species survive intense radiation, heat, and acid, but their resistance mechanisms are often studied in isolation. A systematic, comparative analysis of their genomic repertoires is lacking. This gap limits our understanding of the universal principles of cellular resilience and the evolution of specialized stress response systems, which have implications for astrobiology and biotechnology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extremophiles from distinct phylogenetic lineages surviving similar environmental extremes (e.g., radiation) will share a core set of conserved resistance genes, while also possessing unique, lineage-specific gene families that define their adaptive niches.
proposals.method: We will perform a large-scale pan-genome analysis using pre-2026 tools and databases. Genomes from diverse extremophile genera (e.g., Deinococcus, Thermococcus, Acidithiobacillus) will be clustered into pan-genomes using PanX or Roary. Protein families will be identified and functionally annotated using HMMER against Pfam and TIGRFAMs, with a focus on stress-associated systems (e.g., DNA repair, chaperones, ion transporters). Comparative analysis will employ custom Python/R scripts to identify shared 'core' and 'accessory' resistance gene clusters across stress types, integrating phylogenetic context from GTDB.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first cross-lineage map of extremophile resistance gene families, identifying both universal stress-resistance modules and taxon-specific innovations. It will generate testable predictions about the function of uncharacterized gene clusters in extremophile biology and provide a genomic resource for engineering robust microbial systems.
proposals.novelty: While pan-genome studies are common for pathogens, applying this framework systematically across phylogenetically diverse extremophiles to dissect multi-stress resistance is novel. The approach leverages Aravind's expertise in protein family evolution and stress response systems (e.g., PSP, MutS2) to move beyond single-model-organism studies.

proposals.title: Evolutionary trajectories of caspase-fold specificity: ancestral reconstruction and deep mutational scanning of S1/S1' subsites
proposals.motivation: Caspase-fold proteases are central to apoptosis and inflammation, defined by an almost absolute requirement for aspartate in the P1 position. However, evolution has produced related caspase-fold proteins (e.g., metacaspases, GSDM pore-forming domains) that cleave after non-aspartate residues, suggesting latent evolutionary plasticity. The mechanistic constraints and historical pathways enabling this dramatic shift in specificity remain poorly understood, limiting our ability to predict functional divergence in bacterial virulence-associated caspase-like effectors (e.g., those in systems like the *Legionella* SidE family) and innate immune sensors.
proposals.hypothesis: Ancestral nodes bridging canonical caspases and metacaspases possessed promiscuous or altered cleavage specificities, and the canonical aspartate specificity is maintained by a limited set of structurally constrained residues in the S1 and S1' subsites, mutations in which can functionally rewire specificity.
proposals.method: We will use maximum-likelihood ancestral sequence reconstruction (PhyloBayes, MrBayes) on a curated alignment of caspase-fold homologs to resurrect key ancestral proteins. These will be expressed and screened for activity against synthetic, massively diverse peptide libraries (e.g., using phage display or fluorogenic peptide pools) to empirically map their substrate preferences. In parallel, we will perform deep mutational scanning (using saturation mutagenesis coupled to yeast display or cellular sorting with FRET-based substrate reporters) on the S1 and S1' binding pockets of a model caspase (e.g., caspase-3) to identify all single and double mutants that alter or abolish aspartate specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical map of sequence-function relationships governing caspase-fold specificity, identifying permissible evolutionary paths for specificity switches. It will yield testable models for the evolution of non-apoptotic caspase-fold functions in bacterial virulence and eukaryotic immunity, directly connecting Aravind's expertise in bacterial systems and protein evolution to a central problem in protease biology.
proposals.novelty: While deep mutational scanning has been applied to enzymes, its combination with ancestral reconstruction to explicitly test evolutionary trajectories of protease specificity is novel. This approach moves beyond phylogenetic inference to actively probe the functional landscape of historical sequences, revealing constraints that are invisible in extant proteins.

proposals.title: Defining the Paracaspase Cleavage Motif and Identifying Physiological Substrates in the NF-κB Pathway
proposals.motivation: Paracaspase (MALT1) is a central protease in antigen receptor-mediated NF-κB signaling, yet its precise proteolytic specificity remains poorly defined. While known substrates like A20 and CYLD are cleaved, a comprehensive cleavage motif is lacking, hindering the prediction and validation of novel physiological targets. Defining this specificity is crucial for understanding how paracaspase activity fine-tunes immune and inflammatory responses and for developing targeted therapies for lymphomas driven by MALT1 dysregulation.
proposals.hypothesis: Paracaspase possesses a stringent, definable peptide cleavage preference that can be used to bioinformatically predict and experimentally validate novel physiological substrates involved in NF-κB signaling and immune regulation.
proposals.method: We will employ a multiplexed substrate phage display library to empirically determine the paracaspase cleavage site consensus sequence. Candidate physiological substrates will be predicted using this motif scanned against the human proteome, prioritized by contextual filters (e.g., NF-κB pathway association, protein-protein interaction data). Candidate validation will use in vitro cleavage assays with recombinant proteins and cellular assays in B- and T-cell lines, monitoring cleavage by immunoblot upon pathway stimulation and using catalytically inactive paracaspase mutants as controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-resolution cleavage motif for human paracaspase, providing a critical tool for the field. It will deliver a validated set of novel physiological substrates, expanding the mechanistic understanding of how proteolytic events orchestrate NF-κB signal transduction and potentially revealing new regulatory nodes in immunity and cancer.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior studies focused on individual known substrates, this proposal uses an unbiased systems biology approach—combining empirical enzymology with bioinformatic prediction—to comprehensively define paracaspase specificity and discover its full substrate repertoire, a key gap pre-2026.

proposals.title: Structural and functional mapping of the Bcl10-MALT1 assembly interface for the design of allosteric paracaspase inhibitors
proposals.motivation: The CBM (CARD11-Bcl10-MALT1) signalosome is a critical driver of pathogenic NF-κB signaling in MALT lymphoma and other B-cell malignancies. While catalytic MALT1 inhibitors have been developed, they can disrupt essential immune functions and may not block the non-proteolytic, scaffolding roles of MALT1 that are crucial for oncogenesis. A targeted strategy to disrupt the upstream assembly of the complex represents an unmet therapeutic need with potential for greater specificity.
proposals.hypothesis: High-resolution structural mapping of the Bcl10-MALT1 prodomain interface will reveal a novel, druggable allosteric site on MALT1, and small molecules binding this site can selectively disrupt complex assembly and downstream NF-κB signaling without inhibiting proteolytic activity.
proposals.method: We will employ cryo-EM and X-ray crystallography to determine high-resolution structures of the human Bcl10-MALT1 prodomain complex. Using this structural data, we will perform computational cavity detection and allosteric site prediction with Fpocket and AlloSigMA. Structure-based virtual screening of pre-2026 libraries (e.g., ZINC, Enamine) will be conducted using Glide docking and molecular dynamics simulations with GROMACS to assess binding stability. Top hits will be validated using biochemical assays monitoring Bcl10-MALT1 co-immunoprecipitation and a cell-based NF-κB luciferase reporter assay in MALT lymphoma cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first high-resolution structural model of the Bcl10-MALT1 assembly interface and identify the first-in-class chemical probes that allosterically inhibit MALT1 complex formation. These tools will enable the dissection of the scaffolding versus catalytic functions of MALT1 in oncogenic signaling and establish a new therapeutic paradigm for CBM-dependent lymphomas.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond targeting the conserved catalytic site to instead inhibit the upstream protein-protein interaction that nucleates the complex, a strategy not yet explored for MALT1. It leverages structural biology to target a mechanism (scaffolding) that catalytic inhibitors miss.

proposals.title: Sequence Similarity Network Analysis of the P-loop NTPase Superfamily for the Rational Design of Selective Paracaspase Activity-Based Probes
proposals.motivation: Paracaspase (MALT1) is a key therapeutic target in B-cell lymphomas, but its detection and quantification in vivo is complicated by its membership in the vast P-loop NTPase superfamily, which includes many structurally similar enzymes with conserved nucleotide-binding motifs. Current activity-based probes (ABPs) for paracaspase often lack sufficient selectivity, leading to potential off-target signals and ambiguous readouts in complex tissue environments. A systems-level understanding of sequence and active-site divergence within this superfamily is needed to guide the design of more specific chemical tools.
proposals.hypothesis: Sequence similarity network (SSN) analysis of the P-loop NTPase superfamily will reveal distinct clusters and identify paracaspase-specific active-site residues that can be exploited to design ABPs with enhanced selectivity for active MALT1 over closely related off-target enzymes.
proposals.method: We will construct SSNs using the EFI-EST web tool (pre-2026 standard), applying an iterative alignment score threshold to cluster paracaspases and related P-loop NTPases (e.g., STAND NTPases, other CARMA family members). Conserved active-site features (catalytic cysteine, surrounding loops) will be mapped onto the network using sequence logos and homology models. This map will guide the rational optimization of a fluorescent acyloxymethyl ketone (AOMK) warhead scaffold by introducing steric or electronic modifications predicted to clash with or poorly fit the active sites of off-target clusters, while maintaining affinity for paracaspase.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, network-informed chemical probe with validated specificity for active paracaspase in lymphoma cell lysates and tissue sections. It will also provide a publicly accessible SSN resource for the P-loop NTPase superfamily, mapping functional divergence and offering a generalizable framework for ABP design against targets within large, diverse enzyme families.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond traditional phylogenetics and single-target screening by using a systems biology network visualization (SSN) to explicitly guide the chemical optimization step of ABP development, a strategy not commonly applied to protease/protein families. It directly integrates computational evolutionary analysis with medicinal chemistry for selectivity engineering.

proposals.title: Reconciling the Deep Evolutionary History of Metacaspases and Paracaspases: A Phylogenomic Analysis of the Caspase Superfamily
proposals.motivation: The caspase superfamily, encompassing animal caspases, paracaspases, and metacaspases, is central to eukaryotic programmed cell death and immunity, but its deep evolutionary origins remain contentious. Pre-2026 studies suggest a potential bacterial origin linked to prokaryotic systems like the phage shock protein (PSP) or GATD-containing proteins, yet a rigorous, genome-wide phylogenetic reconciliation that accounts for gene duplication, loss, and horizontal transfer is lacking. Resolving this radiation is critical for understanding the evolutionary assembly of the eukaryotic cell death machinery from potential prokaryotic stress-response components.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a gene-tree/species-tree reconciliation analysis will reveal that the caspase superfamily radiated from a single ancestral gene present in the last eukaryotic common ancestor (LECA), which itself was acquired via horizontal gene transfer from a bacterial progenitor involved in stress response or virulence.
proposals.method: We will construct a comprehensive sequence dataset using sensitive profile-profile searches (HHblits) against genomic and metagenomic databases to identify caspase homologs across eukaryotes, bacteria, and archaea. Maximum-likelihood and Bayesian phylogenies will be inferred using tools like IQ-TREE and PhyloBayes. These gene trees will be reconciled with a robust species tree using probabilistic algorithms in the ALE (Amalgamated Likelihood Estimation) or GeneRax frameworks, explicitly modeling duplication, transfer, and loss (DTL) events to infer the most parsimonious evolutionary scenario at the superfamily's root.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, model-based evolutionary history for the entire caspase superfamily, pinpointing its likely point of origin and major diversification events. It will directly test the proposed links between eukaryotic cell death regulators and specific bacterial systems (e.g., PSP, GATD domains, or virulence-associated proteases), thereby bridging the fields of eukaryotic cell biology and bacterial stress response.
proposals.novelty: While previous studies noted sequence similarities, this proposal applies state-of-the-art phylogenomic reconciliation—a method Aravind's lab has used for systems like MutS2 and C-degron pathways—directly to the caspase superfamily, moving beyond simple tree-building to a statistically rigorous inference of evolutionary events that can distinguish vertical descent from horizontal transfer.

proposals.title: A Systematic Census and Functional Classification of the Caspase-Fold Proteome Across Eukaryotic Lineages
proposals.motivation: The caspase-fold is a versatile structural scaffold found not only in apoptotic caspases but also in diverse regulatory and immune-related proteins like metacaspases, paracaspases, and the bacterial GATD-containing proteins involved in stress response. Pre-2026 studies have identified individual families, but a comprehensive, genome-wide survey across the eukaryotic tree of life is lacking. This systematic identification is crucial for understanding the evolutionary expansion and functional diversification of this fold beyond cell death, potentially linking it to novel signaling pathways, organelle biology, and host-pathogen interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic bioinformatic survey will reveal a significantly larger and more functionally diverse repertoire of caspase-fold proteins in eukaryotes than currently appreciated, including lineage-specific expansions and novel domain architectures that suggest roles in non-apoptotic processes such as organelle regulation and innate immunity.
proposals.method: We will perform sensitive sequence profile searches (using HMMER and PSI-BLAST) and structure-based homology detection (using HHpred) against all sequenced eukaryotic proteomes (e.g., from UniProt, NCBI RefSeq) using known caspase-fold seed alignments. A custom computational pipeline will integrate results, filter false positives, and cluster identified proteins into families. Domain architectures will be analyzed using CDD and Pfam. Phylogenetic analysis of the core fold will be conducted using maximum likelihood methods (e.g., IQ-TREE) to trace evolutionary origins and expansions.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first definitive catalog and phylogenetic classification of the eukaryotic caspase-fold proteome, providing a resource for the community. It will identify novel candidate proteins for biochemical and genetic characterization, potentially revealing new biological functions for this ancient fold. The findings will also inform evolutionary studies on the origins of programmed cell death and related regulatory systems.
proposals.novelty: While previous work focused on specific families (e.g., metacaspases in plants), this study is novel in its systematic, genome-wide scale across all major eukaryotic supergroups. It uniquely integrates Aravind's expertise in bacterial virulence-associated systems (like GATD domains) to search for potential evolutionary connections between prokaryotic and eukaryotic caspase-fold proteins, a largely unexplored angle.

proposals.title: Structural and Mechanistic Elucidation of the Rpn11 JAMM Domain in the 26S Proteasome
proposals.motivation: The JAMM metalloprotease Rpn11 is the essential deubiquitinating enzyme of the 26S proteasome, cleaving ubiquitin chains from substrates prior to their degradation. Despite its critical role in protein homeostasis and cellular regulation, the precise atomic details of its zinc-coordinating active site and substrate recognition mechanism remain poorly understood, hindering targeted modulation. Determining its structure is a fundamental step to understanding proteasomal function and the broader JAMM family, which includes bacterial and viral homologs involved in virulence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the crystal structure of the Rpn11 JAMM domain will reveal a unique arrangement of catalytic residues and a substrate-binding groove that coordinates the ubiquitin chain's isopeptide linkage for hydrolysis. This architecture will explain its specificity and regulation within the proteasome complex.
proposals.method: We will express and purify the human Rpn11 JAMM domain (residues 1-300) and its complex with the Rpn8 subunit. Crystallization trials will employ high-throughput robotic screening, leveraging the researcher's experience with bacterial and phage protein systems. Phasing will be achieved via molecular replacement using known JAMM domain structures (e.g., AMSH) or by experimental methods like selenomethionine MAD. High-resolution data collection will be followed by iterative model building and refinement using Phenix and Coot. Catalytic mechanisms will be probed through mutagenesis of predicted metal-coordinating residues (e.g., Glu, His, Asp).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution structure of the proteasomal Rpn11 JAMM domain, defining the geometry of its catalytic zinc site and the molecular basis for isopeptide bond recognition. It will establish a foundational model for understanding JAMM enzyme evolution and regulation, with implications for diseases of protein misfolding and for bacterial virulence systems that utilize related metalloproteases.
proposals.novelty: While structures of other JAMM domains (e.g., AMSH, BRCC36) exist, the proteasomal Rpn11 structure is unsolved, and its mechanism is inferred. Solving it will reveal how this essential deubiquitinase is uniquely adapted for processive degradation within the massive proteasome complex, a non-obvious integration of metalloprotease chemistry into a macromolecular machine.

proposals.title: Evolutionary trajectory of proteasomal deubiquitination: Tracing the functional specialization of JAMM metalloproteases from archaea to mammals
proposals.motivation: The 26S proteasome's regulatory particle lid, containing the JAMM metalloprotease Rpn11, is essential for deubiquitination prior to substrate degradation. While the core proteasome is well-conserved, the evolutionary path from simple archaeal proteasomes (e.g., PAN complex) to the intricate eukaryotic 26S lid, and how JAMM function scaled with this complexity, remains unclear. Understanding this trajectory is key to deciphering the evolution of regulated protein degradation, a process fundamental to cellular homeostasis and implicated in numerous diseases.
proposals.hypothesis: The deubiquitination activity and regulatory mechanisms of JAMM proteases co-evolved with increasing proteasomal lid complexity, transitioning from a basal, constitutively active function in archaea to a highly regulated, allosterically controlled activity in eukaryotic proteasomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a comparative structural and biochemical approach. First, we will use sensitive sequence profile analysis (e.g., PSI-BLAST, HMMER) and structural modeling (AlphaFold2) to identify and model JAMM proteases and their associated lid subunits across a phylogenetically broad set of species, from archaea to mammals. Second, we will recombinantly express and purify key complexes (e.g., archaeal proteasome-associated JAMM, yeast Rpn11/Rpn8 subcomplex, human lid) for in vitro deubiquitination assays using defined ubiquitin chains. Third, we will use cross-linking mass spectrometry (XL-MS) and targeted mutagenesis to map critical interfaces and test allosteric communication within the evolving lid architecture.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a detailed mechanistic map of how deubiquitination is integrated into the proteasome across evolution. It will identify conserved and divergent features of JAMM regulation, offering new insights into the evolutionary origins of ubiquitin-dependent proteolysis. The findings may reveal ancient regulatory principles that could be exploited for modulating proteasome activity.
proposals.novelty: While JAMM proteases are known, a systematic comparative analysis tracing their functional adaptation alongside the stepwise assembly of the proteasomal lid from archaeal ancestors to mammalian systems has not been conducted, linking Aravind's expertise in protein evolution and systems biology to a core eukaryotic regulatory machine.

ideas.title: Pan-genome analysis of all obligate intracellular pathogens

ideas.sourceTitle: Genome sequence of an obligate intracellular pathogen of humans: Chlamydia trachomatis.

ideas.title: Pan-NTPase classification of pathogenic signaling assemblies, starting with the Bcl10-paracaspase interface
ideas.description: This project fuses large-scale NTPase fold classification with targeted structural biology of a specific disease-linked interface. The goal is to resolve the Bcl10-paracaspase interaction not just as a static structure, but to identify and characterize the specific NTPase fold and catalytic mechanism (if any) that governs its assembly and signal transduction, a question unresolved by either idea alone. By applying the pan-NTPase classification framework to this complex, we can determine if it utilizes a known NTPase superfamily (like AAA+ or kinase) or a novel variant, providing a mechanistic model for its regulation. This establishes a generalizable pipeline to dissect the 'energetic drivers' of other pathogenic signaling assemblies, linking fold evolution directly to disease mechanisms.

ideas.title: Phylogenetic Compressed Sensing for Functional Site Prediction
ideas.description: This idea applies the core principle of compressed sensing from signal processing to the problem of identifying coevolving residues for functional site prediction. In the analogous field, compressed sensing recovers a sparse, high-dimensional signal from limited measurements. Adapted here, the evolutionary histories and sequence variations within a protein family's multiple sequence alignment are treated as an underdetermined linear system, where the 'signal' is the complete set of direct evolutionary couplings between residues. By imposing sparsity constraints (e.g., via LASSO or L1 regularization), the method would efficiently isolate only the strongest, most functionally critical coevolutionary interactions from limited sequence data, reducing noise and computational cost compared to standard Direct Coupling Analysis. This 'phylogenetic compressed sensing' approach aims to more precisely pinpoint the sparse network of residues that define the functional specificity of motifs like the P-loop.

ideas.title: TET catalytic activity dispensable for transcription regulation
ideas.description: TET proteins may regulate transcription through protein-protein interactions independent of their enzymatic oxidase activity.
ideas.sourceTitle: TETonic shift: biological roles of TET proteins in DNA demethylation and transcription

ideas.title: Reconstructing LUCA's NTPome: A Pan-NTPase Phylogenetic Framework for Primordial Energy and Information Processing
ideas.description: This project integrates a comprehensive, domain-wide classification of all major NTPase folds (P-loop, ABC, AAA+, kinases, etc.) with deep phylogenetic analysis and metabolic reconstruction of the Last Universal Common Ancestor (LUCA). By mapping the evolutionary histories of these diverse NTPase superfamilies onto a model of LUCA's core metabolism and information processing pathways, we can systematically deconvolve the primordial 'NTPome'. The goal is to identify which NTPase systems—for energy transduction, signaling, transport, and translation—were integral to LUCA's functional repertoire, providing a mechanistic model for how early life harnessed nucleotide triphosphates. This approach moves beyond single-family studies to reveal the synergistic network of NTP-driven processes that underpinned the emergence of cellular complexity.

ideas.title: Streamlined metabolism masks cryptic pathways
ideas.description: The apparently minimal metabolism may harbor unrecognized enzymatic activities encoded by hypothetical proteins with no known homologs.
ideas.sourceTitle: Complete Genome Sequence of the Apicomplexan, Cryptosporidium parvum

ideas.title: Systems biology modeling of repair networks
ideas.description: Apply computational systems biology to model the integrated DNA repair network from the complete genome data.
ideas.sourceTitle: Genome sequence of the radioresistant bacterium Deinococcus radiodurans R1.

ideas.title: Systematic structural characterization of uncharacterized P-loop families via a census-driven prioritization approach analogous to microbial dark matter genomics
ideas.description: In environmental microbiology, the challenge of characterizing Earth's vast uncultured microbial majority was addressed by the GEBA project, which prioritized organisms for whole-genome sequencing based on phylogenetic distance from known genomes rather than convenience. This maximized novel gene discovery per sequencing effort. An analogous challenge exists in P-loop NTPase biology: dozens of computationally predicted P-loop GTPase families remain structurally and functionally uncharacterized, yet structural genomics consortia have historically selected targets based on tractability or disease relevance rather than evolutionary informativeness. By adapting the GEBA prioritization framework, we propose ranking all predicted but uncharacterized P-loop families by maximum phylogenetic distance from the nearest family with a solved structure. The most divergent families would be prioritized for structure determination using modern high-throughput approaches (AlphaFold-guided construct design, cryo-EM for large complexes, and serial crystallography for difficult targets). Each solved structure would serve as a strong, independent test of whether sequence-based evolutionary classification correctly predicted fold topology and nucleotide-binding mode. This phylogenetically driven campaign would efficiently validate or revise the evolutionary taxonomy of the P-loop superclass while simultaneously maximizing the discovery of novel structural elaborations, catalytic mechanisms, and potential biotechnological applications—mirroring how GEBA's strategic sampling of microbial dark matter reshaped our understanding of prokaryotic diversity.

ideas.title: Network-based deconvolution of vertical and horizontal signals in P-loop GTPase evolution
ideas.description: This project abandons the search for a single tree topology robust to horizontal gene transfer (HGT) and instead aims to explicitly model the network of relationships shaped by both vertical descent and HGT. It employs a suite of phylogenetic and comparative genomic methods to deconstruct the evolutionary history of P-loop GTPases into its vertical and horizontal components, quantifying the relative impact of each process across different prokaryotic lineages. The outcome will be a detailed map of HGT events and conserved vertical cores, providing a more accurate and nuanced representation of GTPase evolution than any single tree could offer. This network-based framework directly addresses the tree-breaking nature of HGT, turning it from a confounding distortion into a central, measurable feature of the analysis.

ideas.title: AlphaFold Structural Phylogeny for P-loop GTPases Using Morphometric Clustering
ideas.description: This idea adapts the paleontological solution of using quantified 3D morphology for classification in the absence of genetic data to the problem of P-loop GTPase evolutionary reclassification. Leveraging the comprehensive set of predicted structures from AlphaFold as a corpus of 3D protein 'morphologies', we will apply geometric morphometric techniques to extract a feature set capturing the spatial configuration of the P-loop, switch regions, and nucleotide-binding elements. By clustering these structural feature vectors using methods like principal component analysis and neighbor-joining, we will construct a phylogeny based solely on structural similarity. This approach aims to reveal deep evolutionary relationships and functional convergences within the P-loop fold that are invisible to sequence-based methods, effectively creating a structural taxonomy for this ancient and diverse protein family.

ideas.title: Pan-proteome AAA+ census across all genomes
ideas.description: Scale whole-genome AAA+ analysis from few genomes to all sequenced organisms for comprehensive evolutionary census.
ideas.sourceTitle: AAA+: A class of chaperone-like ATPases associated with the assembly, operation, and disassembly of protein complexes.

ideas.title: Profile HMM phylogenetics to trace the deep evolutionary history and oncogenic potential of IAP BIR domains
ideas.description: This research applies profile hidden Markov model (HMM) phylogenetic methods, developed for deep protein family analysis in metagenomics, to the BIR (Baculovirus IAP Repeat) domain family. The goal is to reconstruct a high-resolution evolutionary history of BIR domains, identifying deeply branching, sequence-divergent homologs that standard alignment methods miss. This deep evolutionary map will then be used to interpret the oncogenic potential of BIR domains in pathological contexts, such as the IAP2-MALT1 fusion, by assessing whether its pro-survival activity stems from evolutionarily ancient, conserved functions versus recently derived, neomorphic traits. This approach synergistically uses evolutionary discovery to inform mechanistic cancer biology, predicting functional residues and potential interaction partners based on deep conservation patterns.

ideas.title: Coevolution analysis of AAA+ interaction specificity
ideas.description: Apply correlated mutation analysis from protein folding studies to predict AAA+ substrate recognition and partner specificity.
ideas.sourceTitle: AAA+: A class of chaperone-like ATPases associated with the assembly, operation, and disassembly of protein complexes.

ideas.title: Structural Phylogeny of P-loops: A Fold-Centric Census
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that sequence-based classification fully captures the functional diversity within P-loop NTPase families. Drawing an analogy from historical linguistics, where vocabulary-based classification failed to reveal deep language families until the advent of comparative structural grammar, we propose a parallel 'comparative structural grammar' approach for proteins. The analogous solution involved shifting focus from surface features (words/sequences) to underlying structural rules (syntax/fold). Adapted to this context, the research would involve a systematic, computational fold comparison of all proteins containing a Rossmann-like fold to map the structural phylogeny of P-loops. By clustering based on the precise spatial and topological arrangement of the P-loop within its structural context—its 'structural grammar'—we aim to identify structurally divergent yet functionally analogous P-loop members that sequence similarity alone fails to group together, thereby creating a more complete census of this essential functional module.

ideas.title: Ancestral reconstruction and deep mutational scanning of caspase-fold subsites to define evolutionary pathways for altered cleavage specificity
ideas.description: This project directly tests the evolutionary and mechanistic feasibility of alternative cleavage specificities in caspase-fold proteins. We first use ancestral sequence reconstruction to generate plausible historical variants bridging canonical caspases and their non-aspartate-cleaving relatives. These resurrected enzymes are then screened against massively diverse peptide libraries to empirically determine their substrate preferences. Concurrently, we perform deep mutational scanning of the key S1 and S1' subsites to identify mutations that functionally switch specificity, revealing the precise structural constraints and potential evolutionary trajectories for changing the canonical aspartate requirement.

ideas.title: JAMM function across archaeal to mammalian proteasomes
ideas.description: Compare JAMM-dependent deubiquitination across species to trace the evolutionary scaling of lid complexity.
ideas.sourceTitle: Role of Rpn11 Metalloprotease in Deubiquitination and Degradation by the 26S Proteasome

ideas.title: Deep learning-guided discovery of cryptic paracaspase homologs and their pathogenic signaling assemblies
ideas.description: This project uses protein language models to perform a sensitive, structure-aware search for remote homologs of the paracaspase prodomain and Bcl10 across diverse proteomes, focusing on regions critical for their interaction. Identified cryptic homologs are then computationally modeled to predict their heterodimeric interface with Bcl10 or paracaspase, generating testable hypotheses for alternative signaling complexes. This approach moves beyond detecting single proteins to predicting entire, functionally divergent protein interaction modules that could assemble pathogenic signals in lymphoma and related diseases. The final output is a ranked set of predicted non-canonical interaction interfaces for experimental validation, bridging deep learning-based discovery with mechanistic structural biology.

ideas.title: Evolution-Structure Hybrid Network for Robust Remote P-loop Homology Detection
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the pure-sequence limitation of pLMs by creating a dual-stream neural network. One stream processes sequence embeddings from a protein language model to capture deep evolutionary signals, while the other stream analyzes predicted 3D structural features (e.g., backbone geometry, residue proximity, electrostatic potential) of the query. The network is jointly trained to recognize the functional P-loop motif by integrating these complementary information sources, ensuring detection is based on both evolutionary relatedness and conserved structural biophysics. This hybrid system is designed to identify remote homologs where sequence-based methods fail but the core functional architecture remains preserved.

ideas.title: Hidden Markov models for AAA+ detection

ideas.sourceTitle: AAA+: A class of chaperone-like ATPases associated with the assembly, operation, and disassembly of protein complexes.

ideas.title: Context-aware de novo design of synthetic GTPase regulators for a target organism
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the comprehensive, single-organism P-loop GTPase interaction map (Idea A) as a high-fidelity functional context into the inverse prediction engine (Idea B). The goal is to computationally design novel protein sequences predicted not only to perform a desired GTPase regulatory function (e.g., specific GAP or GEF activity) but also to be compatible with the existing interaction network, localization, and signaling logic of the target model organism. This enables the de novo creation of synthetic biological tools and probes that are pre-validated for cellular integration, bridging the gap between in silico sequence design and in vivo functional utility within a defined proteomic environment.

ideas.title: Integrative validation of novel P-loop GTPase subfamilies through phylogenetic robustness filtering, interactome mapping, and domain-swap functional dissection
ideas.description: This study establishes a multi-tiered validation pipeline to distinguish genuine novel P-loop GTPase subfamilies from computational classification artifacts before committing to expensive biochemical characterization. Candidate subfamilies are first stress-tested for phylogenetic robustness using ensemble classification methods, retaining only those consistently identified across algorithms and sequence sampling strategies. Surviving candidates undergo proximity-dependent interactome mapping in relevant cell types to identify subfamily-specific protein interaction networks that would indicate distinct biological roles. Finally, CRISPR-engineered domain-swap constructs replace divergent sequence features of novel subfamilies with canonical GTPase motifs, enabling direct measurement of whether the unique structural elements confer non-redundant cellular functions through phenotypic profiling and GTPase activity assays.

ideas.title: Chaperone function not universal in AAA+
ideas.description: Test whether all AAA+ proteins genuinely perform assembly/disassembly functions or some have entirely novel mechanistic roles.
ideas.sourceTitle: AAA+: A class of chaperone-like ATPases associated with the assembly, operation, and disassembly of protein complexes.

ideas.title: Sequence-based classification captures structural diversity
ideas.description: Test whether sequence-derived P-loop families miss structurally divergent members detectable only by fold comparison.
ideas.sourceTitle: Classification and evolution of P-loop GTPases and related ATPases.

ideas.title: Structure-guided design of allosteric MALT1 inhibitors that disrupt the Bcl10-paracaspase assembly interface
ideas.description: This project integrates high-resolution structural mapping of the Bcl10-MALT1 paracaspase prodomain interface with a structure-based drug discovery pipeline. The goal is to identify and optimize small molecules that bind to a novel allosteric site on MALT1, specifically disrupting its recruitment and activation by Bcl10 without targeting the conserved catalytic cysteine. This approach aims to block pathogenic NF-kappaB signaling in MALT lymphoma with high specificity, potentially overcoming limitations of catalytic inhibitors and providing new chemical probes to dissect the non-proteolytic scaffolding functions of the Bcl10-MALT1 complex.

ideas.title: Characterize TET2 and TET3 enzymatic activity
ideas.description: Determine whether TET2 and TET3 paralogs also catalyze 5mC oxidation with distinct tissue specificities.
ideas.sourceTitle: Conversion of 5-Methylcytosine to 5-Hydroxymethylcytosine in Mammalian DNA by MLL Partner TET1

ideas.title: Minimal Functional GTPase Design via Synthetic Biology Circuit Principles
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by applying an analogy from synthetic biology's approach to designing minimal functional gene circuits. The analogous problem—distilling a complex biological function (like oscillation or toggle switching) down to its smallest set of genetic components—is solved by identifying essential regulatory parts and their minimal connections. Adapting this solution, we reconceptualize a P-loop GTPase not as a monolithic protein fold to be trimmed, but as a functional 'circuit' composed of core modules: the G-domain with its P-loop (catalytic processor), the switch I and II regions (conformational output), and a minimal, high-affinity binding motif for a GAP protein (essential regulatory input). The research will involve computational design to integrate these modules into a minimal polypeptide, potentially using de novo scaffolds or ultra-short linkers, followed by experimental validation of GTP binding, hydrolysis, and GAP-dependent switching. The goal is to create the smallest possible functional unit that retains the core mechanistic logic of a GTPase.

ideas.title: Network-Guided Design of Paracaspase Probes Using Sequence Similarity Networks
ideas.description: This research direction fuses activity-based probe (ABP) development with systems biology network analysis. It applies sequence similarity networks (SSNs) to visualize the relationships within the P-loop NTPase superfamily, which includes paracaspases, to move beyond simple phylogenetic trees. The network analysis identifies clusters of closely related enzymes and maps conserved versus divergent active-site features. This map is then used to rationally design and optimize fluorescent ABPs that are maximally selective for active paracaspase in lymphoma tissues, while minimizing off-target binding to other structurally similar P-loop family members, thereby improving in vivo detection specificity and quantification.

ideas.title: Structural genomics pipelines for AAA+ classification
ideas.description: Apply high-throughput structural genomics approaches to solve multiple AAA+ structures simultaneously for systematic classification.
ideas.sourceTitle: AAA+: A class of chaperone-like ATPases associated with the assembly, operation, and disassembly of protein complexes.

ideas.title: Characterize paracaspase proteolytic substrate specificity
ideas.description: Determine the cleavage site preferences and natural substrates of paracaspase to understand its enzymatic role in NF-kappaB signaling.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of paracaspases and metacaspases: two ancient families of caspase-like proteins, one of which plays a key role in MALT lymphoma.

ideas.title: Pan-NTPase classification across all domains
ideas.description: Scale classification to all NTPase folds beyond P-loop, including ABC, AAA+, and kinase superfamilies.
ideas.sourceTitle: Classification and evolution of P-loop GTPases and related ATPases.

ideas.title: Phylogenomic reconciliation of caspase superfamily evolution
ideas.description: Apply molecular phylogenetics with gene-tree species-tree reconciliation to resolve the evolutionary origin of the caspase superfamily radiation.
ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of paracaspases and metacaspases: two ancient families of caspase-like proteins, one of which plays a key role in MALT lymphoma.

ideas.title: Semantic Search for Remote Homology Detection Using Protein Language Model Embeddings
ideas.description: This idea adapts the semantic search paradigm from Natural Language Processing to the problem of detecting remote protein homology. In NLP, keyword-based search (analogous to PSI-BLAST's sequence-based search) fails when documents share meaning but not specific terms. This is solved by using dense vector embeddings from models like BERT to capture semantic context, followed by efficient approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) search in the embedding space to find semantically similar documents. We adapt this solution by using a deep learning protein language model (e.g., ESM, ProtTrans) to generate dense, contextual embeddings for all proteins in a large, diverse database (e.g., UniRef). For a query P-loop protein, we compute its embedding and use an ANN library (e.g., FAISS, HNSW) to rapidly find the nearest neighbor embeddings in the database. This approach searches for homology based on deep functional and structural patterns learned by the pLM, potentially uncovering remote P-loop homologs that share a common evolutionary origin but have diverged beyond the detection threshold of iterative, profile-based methods like PSI-BLAST.

ideas.title: Iterative, alignment-free guided HMM phylogenetics for unbiased deep NTPase evolution
ideas.description: This approach overcomes model bias by first applying alignment-free sequence analysis to massive, diverse metagenomic NTPase datasets to identify novel clusters and sequence space without a priori alignment assumptions. These clusters guide the creation of multiple, targeted profile HMMs, ensuring model diversity. Phylogenetic trees are then inferred using each model and compared for congruence, with a final consensus topology built only from robust, model-invariant relationships. This system explicitly tests and mitigates the bias inherent in single-model HMM phylogenetics, leading to a more robust and discovery-oriented map of deep P-loop NTPase evolution.

ideas.title: A Unified Periodic Table of NTPase Folds
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to scale NTPase classification to all folds by drawing an analogy to the development of the Periodic Table in chemistry. The analogous solution was the identification of a fundamental, ordering principle (atomic number) that organized all known and predicted elements based on core properties, revealing periodic trends. Adapted to NTPases, the goal is to move beyond sequence- and fold-based phylogenies to discover a comparable foundational principle—potentially the precise geometry and electrostatics of the catalytic metal ion coordination sphere—that is conserved across all NTPase folds. By systematically characterizing this 'coordination signature' across superfamilies (P-loop, ABC, AAA+, kinases, and others), we aim to construct a unified, predictive 'Periodic Table of NTPase Folds.' This framework would classify known NTPases, predict functions of uncharacterized members, and reveal evolutionary relationships transcending traditional fold boundaries, achieving a pan-domain scaling of classification based on first mechanistic principles.

ideas.title: Yeast two-hybrid screens for metacaspase interactors

ideas.sourceTitle: Identification of paracaspases and metacaspases: two ancient families of caspase-like proteins, one of which plays a key role in MALT lymphoma.

ideas.title: Pan-NTPase fold analysis of IAP2-BIR domains reveals novel, targetable nucleotide-dependent apoptosis inhibition mechanisms
ideas.description: This research direction applies the comprehensive structural and mechanistic framework of pan-NTPase classification to the BIR domains of IAP2 found in oncogenic fusions. By analyzing IAP2-BIR domains not just as apoptosis inhibitors but as potential members of specific NTPase folds (e.g., akin to nucleotide-sensing regulatory modules in AAA+ or kinase superfamilies), we can search for cryptic nucleotide-binding sites or allosteric regulatory mechanisms that contribute to its caspase-independent oncogenicity. This enables the prediction of novel, druggable pockets or conformational states unique to the fusion protein's BIR domains that would be invisible to standard homology-based approaches. The ultimate goal is to identify small molecules that disrupt these newly classified NTPase-like functions, offering a targeted strategy to counteract IAP2-MALT1 fusion-driven lymphomagenesis.

ideas.title: In vivo split-luciferase screening for context-dependent metacaspase interactomes in plants and fungi
ideas.description: This redesigned approach uses a protein-fragment complementation assay (PCA) based on split luciferase to map metacaspase protein-protein interactions within the native cellular environment of plant or fungal cells. By screening a cDNA library fused to one luciferase fragment against the metacaspase fused to the other fragment directly in protoplasts or intact cells, it captures interactions that depend on specific subcellular localization, proteolytic activation, or other post-translational modifications present only in the host organism. This context-aware screening minimizes false positives from the heterologous yeast system and reveals condition-specific interaction networks, such as those induced by stress or during programmed cell death, providing a more physiologically relevant and functionally informative interactome.

ideas.title: Unbiased functional profiling and interactome mapping of Dictyostelium paracaspase to define its role in development.
ideas.description: This study first performs an unbiased functional characterization of the Dictyostelium paracaspase to avoid presupposing its role. A knockout strain is generated and subjected to detailed phenotyping across growth, aggregation, and multicellular development stages, while its native protein interaction network is mapped via affinity purification-mass spectrometry. This foundational data identifies all potential functional pathways linked to the paracaspase. Subsequently, specific, high-resolution assays for programmed cell death are applied within the developmental context to rigorously test whether PCD is a primary, secondary, or absent function of the protein, ensuring the findings accurately reflect its true biological role.

ideas.title: Metacaspases as 'Molecular Swiss Army Knives': Re-evaluating Function via a Neurotransmitter Analogy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that metacaspases are primarily executors of programmed cell death in plants and microbes, analogous to how neuroscience moved beyond viewing glutamate solely as an excitotoxic killer. In neuroscience, the solution was to dissect context—different receptors and pathways—that allowed glutamate to function in vital plasticity or pathological death. Adapting this solution, we propose that metacaspase function is dictated by specific protein-protein interactions and substrate cleavage profiles dependent on cellular context. The research would involve comparative proteomics and interactome analysis under varying conditions to rigorously distinguish metacaspase complexes and activities associated with housekeeping regulatory processes (like selective protein degradation or stress signaling) from those uniquely associated with the terminal events of cell death, thereby reframing them as multifunctional signaling proteases rather than dedicated executioners.

ideas.title: Quantitative Network Topology Features Enable Predictive Classification of P-loop NTPase Families
ideas.description: This approach constructs a sequence similarity network for the P-loop fold but then extracts quantitative topological descriptors (e.g., betweenness centrality, modularity class strength, edge weight distribution) for each node (protein). These network-based features, combined with sequence-derived features, are used to train supervised classifiers (e.g., random forests or SVMs) to predict functional classes, mechanistic attributes, or evolutionary relationships. The system directly tests whether network structure contains predictive signal absent from sequence alone, moving from visualization to falsifiable, quantitative classification. This creates a robust framework that can outperform models based solely on sequence homology or phylogenetic trees for identifying convergent evolution and functional divergence.

ideas.title: Decoupling Scaffold from Protease: Orthogonal Validation of Catalysis-Independent Paracaspase Signaling Complexes
ideas.description: This redesigned study directly tests the catalysis-independent scaffolding hypothesis by engineering a paracaspase mutant with dual-point mutations that ablate both catalytic activity and substrate binding, eliminating confounding proteolytic functions. This construct will be tested in a reconstituted signaling system, such as the CBM complex in lymphocytes, using binding assays and functional readouts like NF-κB activation. Crucially, rescue experiments will be performed in paracaspase-deficient cells using this dead mutant versus a wild-type control to determine if the mutant can restore specific signaling outputs, thereby proving a true scaffolding role separable from proteolysis.

ideas.title: Functionally validated census of active viral P-loop GTPases
ideas.description: This project moves beyond a sequence catalog to establish a validated census of functionally active viral GTPases. Candidate domains identified through mining viral genomes are filtered through a pipeline assessing structural integrity, motif conservation, and genomic context. High-priority candidates are then expressed and screened using a high-throughput fluorescence-based GTPase assay to biochemically confirm enzymatic activity. The final output is a rigorously curated database linking sequence diversity to experimentally verified function, providing a robust foundation for studying viral GTPase evolution and mechanism.

ideas.title: Constrained Generative Design of Functional GTPase Regulators via Multi-Objective Inverse Folding
ideas.description: This approach inverts the function-to-sequence mapping by employing a generative model trained to produce amino acid sequences that satisfy multiple simultaneous constraints. The model is conditioned not only on the desired GTPase regulatory function but also on explicit biophysical objectives for protein stability and co-evolutionary signatures, ensuring foldability. Generated sequences are then ranked by a confidence score integrating the functional prediction likelihood and the satisfaction of these auxiliary constraints. This results in a focused, high-confidence set of candidate sequences for experimental testing, dramatically increasing the likelihood of successful functional validation.

ideas.title: A High-Resolution, Contextualized Interaction Atlas for a Single Organism's P-loop GTPase Network
ideas.description: Scale down from cross-species classification to map all P-loop GTPase interactions within one model organism. Drawing an analogy from urban transportation network mapping, this research will not produce a simple list of interactions but a high-resolution, contextualized atlas. By integrating diverse datasets—including protein-protein interaction assays, genetic interaction screens, spatial proteomics, and cell cycle/metabolic state information—into a unified model, the project aims to reveal the dynamic functional logic, regulatory bottlenecks, and system-level interdependencies of the complete P-loop GTPase network operating within a single, living cellular 'city'.

ideas.title: Determine the oligomeric Bcl10 filament-paracaspase interface driving pathogenic CBM signalosome assembly
ideas.description: This project aims to resolve the structural basis of paracaspase recruitment and activation by the oligomeric Bcl10 filament, the core signaling platform in the CBM complex. Using cryo-EM, we will determine the structure of a reconstituted Bcl10 filament bound to the paracaspase prodomain to capture the multivalent interactions critical for signal amplification. Functional validation through interface disruption in lymphocyte cell lines will directly test the necessity of the observed contacts for NF-κB activation. This approach moves beyond a minimal binary interface to reveal the assembly mechanism underlying constitutive signaling in MALT lymphoma.

ideas.title: Adapting One-Bead-One-Compound (OBOC) Library Screening from Drug Discovery for Paracaspase Substrate Profiling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the One-Bead-One-Compound (OBOC) library screening methodology, a cornerstone technique in medicinal chemistry for identifying protein-binding ligands, to the problem of systematically profiling the cleavage preferences of paracaspases and metacaspases. In the analogous solution, vast libraries of diverse compounds are synthesized on individual beads, screened against a labeled protein target, and active beads are isolated and decoded. We adapt this by synthesizing combinatorial peptide substrate libraries on beads, where cleavage by a fluorescently labeled paracaspase/metacaspase alters the bead's fluorescent properties or releases a capturable tag. Positively selected 'hit' beads are then isolated, and the peptide sequence is determined via Edman degradation or mass spectrometry, enabling high-throughput, unbiased identification of optimal cleavage sequences and the systematic mapping of subsite preferences (P4-P4') for these enzymes.
