ideas.title: Adapting orphan receptor deorphanization strategies to functionally characterize novel GTPase subfamilies
ideas.description: This approach draws an analogy from the systematic deorphanization of GPCRs in pharmacology, where hundreds of genomically predicted but functionally uncharacterized receptors were matched to their ligands and signaling pathways through reverse pharmacology and high-throughput screening. Similarly, computationally predicted novel P-loop GTPase subfamilies represent 'orphan enzymes' with no known biochemical function, interaction partners, or cellular roles. The adapted strategy involves: (1) expressing and purifying representative members of each novel GTPase subfamily; (2) profiling their intrinsic nucleotide hydrolysis activity across GTP, ATP, and other nucleotide substrates using real-time fluorescence assays; (3) screening against arrayed libraries of known GEFs, GAPs, and effector proteins from characterized GTPase families, prioritized by phylogenetic distance; (4) measuring constitutive versus stimulated activity to identify subfamilies with unusual regulatory properties; and (5) validating biochemical hits with cellular localization, knockout phenotyping, and pathway perturbation experiments. This systematic 'deorphanization' pipeline would convert computationally predicted GTPase diversity into experimentally grounded functional annotations, paralleling how reverse pharmacology transformed orphan GPCR biology into actionable drug target knowledge.

ideas.title: Develop covalent MALT1 inhibitors using fragment-based electrophile tethering
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from cysteine protease inhibitor design in virology/parasitology—specifically, the fragment-based 'electrophile tethering' approach used to develop covalent inhibitors for viral proteases like SARS-CoV-2 Mpro. The analogous solution involved screening reversible binding fragments near the catalytic cysteine, then chemically linking them to an irreversible electrophilic warhead to achieve potent and selective covalent inhibition. Adapted to this context, the idea is to design small-molecule inhibitors for MALT1 paracaspase by first identifying reversible fragments that bind its active site, then using structural data to tether these fragments to a cysteine-reactive electrophile (e.g., acrylamide) via an optimized linker. This strategy aims to generate covalent inhibitors that selectively and irreversibly block the catalytic cysteine of MALT1, thereby potently inhibiting its protease activity and the downstream oncogenic NF-kappaB signaling driving MALT lymphoma.

ideas.title: Evolutionarily Validated Minimal GTPase Units via Continuous Selection for Allosteric Cycling
ideas.description: This project redesigns the minimal GTPase concept to directly solve the problem of uncoupling structure from mechanism. Instead of a purely structure-based minimization, it employs a continuous directed evolution system where libraries of truncated GTPase genes are linked to the survival of host cells via a FRET-based biosensor that reports on successful switch-I/II conformational cycling. Only variants that repeatedly hydrolyze GTP and undergo the associated conformational changes will propagate. This approach guarantees that the resulting minimal units are not just stable scaffolds but are evolutionarily validated as functional, dynamic nanomachines, revealing the indispensable core for mechanochemical coupling.

ideas.title: Inverse Protein Design via Generative Adversarial Networks
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the inverse design problem from computational materials science to molecular biology. In materials science, Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) are used to solve the inverse problem of designing novel atomic compositions that yield a target material property. Here, we propose inverting the GTPase regulatory function classification problem by implementing a similar GAN architecture. A generator network will be trained to propose novel protein sequences, while a discriminator network—initialized from the pre-trained forward model that classifies sequences by their regulatory function—will act as an adversarial critic, evaluating the functional plausibility of the generated sequences. This adversarial training loop will iteratively refine the generator to produce novel, non-natural amino acid sequences predicted to possess the desired GTPase regulatory function, effectively scaling the search for functional sequences beyond known evolutionary space.

ideas.title: Acute functional interrogation of metacaspase necessity in HR-PCD execution via chemical-genetic ablation
ideas.description: This study directly tests the necessity of specific plant metacaspases for hypersensitive response programmed cell death (HR-PCD) execution by combining genetic and acute pharmacological inhibition. Using Arabidopsis lines expressing a dexamethasone-inducible avrRpt2 effector to synchronously trigger HR, we will apply a newly synthesized, plant-optimized metacaspase-specific covalent inhibitor at precise timepoints post-induction. This acute blockade, complemented by analysis of CRISPR-generated catalytic dead mutants, will determine if metacaspase activity is required for the point-of-no-return in cell death, distinguishing execution from upstream signaling or parallel processes. The approach will define the temporal window of metacaspase necessity and identify direct in vivo substrates via comparison of proteomes from inhibitor-treated and control tissues during HR progression.
